tEn: Elon Musk on AI, robots, and the future
tZh: 埃隆·马斯克谈 AI、机器人与未来
sEn: A marathon conversation spanning xAI and Grok, Optimus, Mars, and AI risk.
sZh: 一场马拉松式长谈,横跨 xAI 与 Grok、Optimus、火星,以及 AI 风险。

consensus.en: AI safety requires maximally truth-seeking AI, not forced false beliefs.
consensus.zh: AI安全需要最大程度追求真相的AI，而非被迫接受错误信念。

consensus.en: Full rocket reusability drops space access cost by 100x or more.
consensus.zh: 火箭完全可重复使用可将太空访问成本降低100倍或更多。

consensus.en: Government fraud is massive; requiring payment codes and comments can stop billions in waste.
consensus.zh: 政府欺诈规模巨大；要求支付代码和备注可阻止数十亿浪费。

consensus.en: Open borders and mass immigration are used to import voters for political power.
consensus.zh: 开放边境和大规模移民被用来引进选民以获取政治权力。

consensus.en: The woke mind virus in AI could lead to dystopian outcomes if unchecked.
consensus.zh: AI中的觉醒病毒若不加控制可能导致反乌托邦后果。

consensus.en: AI and robotics are the only way to solve the national debt crisis.
consensus.zh: AI和机器人技术是解决国家债务危机的唯一途径。

contrarian.en: The most interesting outcome is most likely in a simulation, as boring ones are terminated.
contrarian.zh: 在模拟中，最有趣的结果最可能发生，因为无聊的会被终止。

contrarian.en: Capitalism, not socialism, will achieve universal high income via AI and robotics.
contrarian.zh: 资本主义而非社会主义将通过AI和机器人实现全民高收入。

contrarian.en: Social Security database has 20 million people who cannot possibly be alive, enabling fraud.
contrarian.zh: 社保数据库中有2000万不可能活着的人，助长了欺诈。

contrarian.en: The Democratic party aims to destroy democracy by importing voters, not saving it.
contrarian.zh: 民主党旨在通过引进选民摧毁民主，而非拯救它。

contrarian.en: Transing kids increases suicide risk threefold, contrary to claims of saving them.
contrarian.zh: 让孩子变性使自杀风险增加三倍，与拯救他们的说法相反。

contrarian.en: The Biden White House delayed rescuing astronauts for political reasons before the election.
contrarian.zh: 拜登白宫因政治原因在大选前推迟救援宇航员。

turns.en: It's every day. And every day it's like more and more ridiculous to the point where the simulation is more and more undeniable.
turns.zh: 每天如此。而且每天越来越荒谬，以至于模拟越来越不可否认。

turns.en: Yeah. Yeah. It really feels like simulation, you know? It's like, come on. What are the odds that this could be the case?
turns.zh: 是啊。真的感觉像模拟，你知道吗？就像，拜托。这种情况的概率有多大？

turns.en: Are you paying attention at all to Three Atlas? Are you watching the comet?
turns.zh: 你有关注 Three Atlas 吗？你在看那颗彗星吗？

turns.en: Yeah. Whatever it is.
turns.zh: 是啊。不管它是什么。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, one thing I can say is like, look, if I was aware of any evidence of aliens, Joe, you have my word. I will come on your show and I will reveal it on the show.
turns.zh: 是啊。我是说，有一件事我可以这么说，听着，如果我知道任何外星人的证据，Joe，我向你保证。我会来你的节目，并在节目中揭露它。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: Yeah, that's a good deal.
turns.zh: 是啊，这是个好交易。

turns.en: Yeah, it's pretty good.
turns.zh: 是啊，挺不错的。

turns.en: I'll believe you. Yeah, thank you.
turns.zh: 我相信你。好的，谢谢。

turns.en: I'll stick to my promises. So, all right. I'll hold you to that.
turns.zh: 我会信守承诺的。所以，好的。我会让你兑现的。

turns.en: Yeah. And I'm never committing suicide, to be clear.
turns.zh: 是啊。而且我永远不会自杀，说清楚。

turns.en: I don't think you would either.
turns.zh: 我也不认为你会。

turns.en: So, on camera, guys, I am never committing suicide ever.
turns.zh: 所以，在镜头前，伙计们，我永远不会自杀。

turns.en: If someone says you committed suicide, I will fight tooth and nail. I will not believe it.
turns.zh: 如果有人说你自杀了，我会全力抗争。我不会相信的。

turns.en: The thing about the Three Atlas is it's a hell of a name actually.
turns.zh: 关于 Three Atlas 的事，这名字其实挺酷的。

turns.en: Yeah, it's a third eye sounds like third eye or something.
turns.zh: 是啊，第三只眼，听起来像第三只眼之类的。

turns.en: Yeah, it does. Three Atlas is the third interstellar object that's detected.
turns.zh: 是啊，确实。Three Atlas 是探测到的第三个星际天体。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: Yeah. Avi Loeb was on the podcast a couple days ago talking about it.
turns.zh: 是啊。Avi Loeb 几天前在播客上谈过这个。

turns.en: Yeah. It could be. I don't know. But apparently today they're saying that it's changed course. Did you see that, Jamie?
turns.zh: 是啊。有可能。我不知道。但显然今天他们说它改变了轨道。你看到了吗，Jamie？

turns.en: Avi said something today. I'll send it to you. I know it's on Reddit.
turns.zh: Avi 今天说了些什么。我发给你。我知道在 Reddit 上。

turns.en: Here you go, Jamie. I'll send it to you right now. It's fascinating. It's fascinating also because it's made almost entirely of nickel, whatever it is. And the only way that exists here is industrial alloys apparently.
turns.zh: 给你，Jamie。我现在就发给你。这很迷人。也很迷人因为它几乎完全由镍构成，不管它是什么。而这里存在的唯一方式显然是工业合金。

turns.en: Most no, there are definitely comets and asteroids that are made primarily of nickel in fact. The places where you mine nickel on Earth are actually where an asteroid or comet hit Earth that was a nickel-rich deposit. Those are from impacts. You definitely didn't want to be there at the time because anything would have been obliterated. That's where the sources of nickel and cobalt are these days.
turns.zh: 大多数不是，但确实有一些彗星和小行星主要由镍构成。地球上开采镍的地方，实际上就是富含镍的小行星或彗星撞击地球形成的矿床。那些都来自撞击。你肯定不想当时在场，因为任何东西都会被摧毁。如今镍和钴的来源就在那里。

turns.en: So, this is Ovi O Lope. A few hours ago, the first hint of non-gravitational acceleration was indicated, meaning something other than gravity is affecting its trajectory. Interesting. So it's mostly nickel, very little iron, which on Earth only exists in alloys, but you're dealing with another planet.
turns.zh: 所以，这就是 Ovi O Lope。几小时前，首次出现了非引力加速度的迹象，意味着除了引力之外还有别的东西在影响它的轨迹。有意思。所以它主要是镍，铁很少，而铁在地球上只以合金形式存在，但你面对的是另一颗行星。

turns.en: There are cases where there are very nickel-rich asteroids or meteorites that are heavy, something from space.
turns.zh: 有些情况是，存在非常富含镍的小行星或陨石，很重，来自太空。

turns.en: Yeah, it doesn't mean it'll be a very heavy spaceship if you make it all out of nickel. Oh yeah. And huge. The size of Manhattan and all nickel. That's kind of nuts.
turns.zh: 是啊，但如果你全用镍造飞船，不一定就会很重。哦对。而且巨大，曼哈顿那么大，全是镍。那有点疯狂。

turns.en: Yeah, that's a heavy spaceship.
turns.zh: 是啊，那是艘很重的飞船。

turns.en: That's a real problem if it hits.
turns.zh: 如果它撞上来，那真是个大问题。

turns.en: Yes. No, it would obliterate a continent type of thing.
turns.zh: 是的。不，它会摧毁一个大陆之类的东西。

turns.en: Maybe worse.
turns.zh: 可能更糟。

turns.en: Probably kill most of human life, if not all of us. It depends on the total mass. In the fossil record, there are arguably five major extinction events. The biggest one is the Permian extinction, where almost all life was eliminated, but that occurred over several million years. The Jurassic one is pretty definitively an asteroid. But there have been five major extinction events, but they don't count the ones that merely take out a continent.
turns.zh: 可能会杀死大部分人类，甚至全部。取决于总质量。在化石记录中，可以说有五次大灭绝事件。最大的一次是二叠纪灭绝，几乎消灭了所有生命，但那持续了几百万年。侏罗纪那次很确定是小行星。但发生过五次大灭绝事件，但他们不算那些仅仅摧毁一个大陆的事件。

turns.en: So merely.
turns.zh: 所以仅仅是。

turns.en: Yeah, because those don't show up in the fossil record unless it's enough to cause a mass extinction event throughout Earth. So there have been many impacts that would have destroyed all life on, say, half of North America. There have been many such impacts through the course of history.
turns.zh: 是的，因为除非足以引发全球性大灭绝，否则那些不会出现在化石记录中。所以历史上有很多撞击会摧毁比如半个北美大陆的所有生命。这样的撞击在历史进程中发生过很多次。

turns.en: Yeah. And there's nothing we can do about it right now.
turns.zh: 是啊。而且我们现在对此无能为力。

turns.en: Yeah. There was one that hit Siberia and destroyed a few hundred square miles.
turns.zh: 是的。有一次撞击西伯利亚，摧毁了几百平方英里。

turns.en: Oh, that's the Tunguska.
turns.zh: 哦，那是通古斯事件。

turns.en: Yeah. That's the one from the 1920s, right?
turns.zh: 是的。就是1920年代那次，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah. That's the one that coincides with the meteor or comet storm that we go through every June and every November, which they think is responsible for that younger dryas.
turns.zh: 是的。就是那个与我们每年六月和十一月经历的流星或彗星风暴吻合的事件，他们认为那是新仙女木期的原因。

turns.en: Yeah. All that stuff is crazy.
turns.zh: 是啊。所有这些都很疯狂。

turns.en: Thank you before we go any further for letting us have a tour of SpaceX and letting us be there for the rocket launch. One of the absolute coolest things I've ever seen in my life. We thought it was only half a mile, Jamie thought it was a mile away, turned out it's almost two miles away. And you feel it in your chest. You have to wear earplugs and you feel it in your chest and it's 2 miles away. It was amazing. And then to go with you up into the command center and to watch all the Starlink satellites with all the different cameras and all in real time as it made its way all the way to Australia. How many minutes? Like 35, 40 minutes. Wild it touched down in Australia. Crazy. It was amazing. Absolutely amazing. The Starship's awesome.
turns.zh: 在我们继续之前，感谢你让我们参观 SpaceX，并让我们在现场观看火箭发射。这是我一生中见过的最酷的事情之一。我们以为只有半英里远，杰米以为是一英里，结果差不多有两英里远。你能在胸口感受到震动。你必须戴耳塞，在两英里外都能在胸口感受到。太神奇了。然后和你一起进入指挥中心，观看所有星链卫星，通过不同摄像头实时看到它一路飞到澳大利亚。多少分钟？大概35、40分钟。它降落在澳大利亚，太疯狂了。太棒了。绝对精彩。星舰太厉害了。

turns.en: Yeah. Anyone can go watch the launch actually. You can just go to South Padre Island, it has a great view of the launch. It's where a lot of spring breakers go. But we'll be flying pretty frequently out of Starbase in South Texas. We formally incorporated it as a city. So it's actually a legally actual city, Starbase, Texas. It's not that often you hear like, hey, we made a city. That used to be like in the old days, a startup would be you go and gather a bunch of people and say, 'Hey, let's go make a town.' Literally, that would have been startups in the old days.
turns.zh: 是的。其实任何人都可以去看发射。你可以去南帕德里岛，那里看发射视野很好。很多春假游客都去那里。但我们会频繁地从南德克萨斯州的星城基地发射。我们正式把它设为一个城市。所以它实际上是一个合法的城市，德克萨斯州的星城。你不常听到有人说，嘿，我们建了个城市。在过去，创业公司就是召集一群人，说‘嘿，我们去建个镇吧’。 literally，那就是过去的创业方式。

turns.en: Or a country.
turns.zh: 或者一个国家。

turns.en: Yeah. Or a country.
turns.zh: 是的。或者一个国家。

turns.en: Yeah. If you tried doing that today, there'd be a real problem. Things are so set in stone on the country front these days. You might pull it off if you got a solid island. You know, like at owns lai.
turns.zh: 是啊。如果今天你尝试那样做，会有大问题。如今国家方面的事情太固定了。如果你有一个稳固的岛屿，也许能成功。你知道，就像 at owns lai。

turns.en: Yeah, you could probably if you put enough effort into it, you could make a new country.
turns.zh: 是的，如果你投入足够努力，也许能建立一个新的国家。

turns.en: This is one of the different ones. This is one of the ones that you catch, right? Or is that one?
turns.zh: 这个是不同的。这是你要接住的那个，对吧？还是那个？

turns.en: Yeah, that's the booster. So that's the Super Heavy booster. It has 33 engines. By version four, it will have about 10,000 tons of thrust. Right now it's about 7,000 to 8,000 tons of thrust. That's the largest flying object ever made.
turns.zh: 是的，那是助推器。那就是超重型助推器。它有33台发动机。到第四版时，它将有大约10,000吨推力。目前大约有7,000到8,000吨推力。这是有史以来最大的飞行器。

turns.en: I had to explain to someone. They were going, 'Why do they blow up all the time if he's so smart?' Because there was this idiot on television. Some guy was being interviewed and they were talking about you. And he goes, 'Oh, I think he's a fuckwit.' And he goes, 'Why do his rockets keep blowing up?' And I had to explain. Yeah. Because it's the only way you find out what the tolerances are. You have to test a few corners of the box.
turns.zh: 我不得不向某人解释。他们问，‘如果他那么聪明，为什么火箭总是爆炸？’因为电视上有个白痴。有个人在接受采访，他们谈到你。他说，‘哦，我觉得他是个傻逼。’他说，‘为什么他的火箭老是爆炸？’我不得不解释。是的。因为那是找出公差极限的唯一方法。你必须测试几个边界情况。

turns.en: So when you do a new rocket development program, you have to do what's called exploring the limits, the corners of the box where you say worst case this, worst case that, to figure out where the limits are. So you blow up, not admittedly in the development process sometimes blows up accidentally.
turns.zh: 所以当你进行新的火箭开发项目时，你必须做所谓的探索极限，即边界情况，你说最坏情况这样，最坏情况那样，以找出极限在哪里。所以你会爆炸，当然在开发过程中有时也会意外爆炸。

turns.en: But we intentionally subject it to a flight regime that is much worse than what we expect in normal flight, so that when we put people on board or valuable cargo, it doesn't blow up. For example, for the flight you saw, we deliberately took heat shield tiles off the Starship in some of the worst locations to see if losing a tile there is catastrophic or not. Nonetheless, Starship was able to do a soft landing in the Indian Ocean west of Australia. It got there from Texas in about 354 minutes.
turns.zh: 但我们故意让它经历比正常飞行恶劣得多的飞行状态，这样当我们载人或有贵重货物时，它不会爆炸。例如，在你看到的那次飞行中，我们故意在一些最糟糕的位置拆掉了星舰的隔热瓦，看看失去一块瓦片是否会造成灾难性后果。尽管如此，星舰还是成功在印度洋澳大利亚以西实现了软着陆。从德克萨斯到那里大约用了354分钟。

turns.en: So it landed even though you put it through this situation where it has compromised shield.
turns.zh: 所以它即使在你让它处于隔热层受损的情况下还是着陆了。

turns.en: It had an unusually hot trajectory with missing tiles to see if it would still make a soft landing, which it did. It did have some holes burnt into it, but it was robust enough to land despite that, because it's coming in like a blazing meteor. You can see the real-time video.
turns.zh: 它走了一条异常高温的轨迹，还缺了隔热瓦，就是为了看看它是否还能软着陆，结果它做到了。它确实被烧出了一些洞，但足够坚固，还是着陆了，因为它像一颗燃烧的流星一样飞进来。你可以看到实时视频。

turns.en: Tell me the speed again because it was bananas.
turns.zh: 再告诉我一次速度，因为那太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah, it's about 17,000 mph, like 25 times the speed of sound. So think of it as 12 times faster than a bullet from an assault rifle, which is around Mach 2. Or compared to a bullet from a handgun, which is subsonic, it's about 30 times faster. And it's huge.
turns.zh: 是的，大约每小时17000英里，也就是25倍音速。所以可以想象它比突击步枪子弹快12倍，步枪子弹大约2马赫。或者与手枪子弹相比，手枪子弹是亚音速的，它大约快30倍。而且它非常巨大。

turns.en: 30 times faster than a bullet from a handgun and it's the size of a skyscraper. That's fast. It's so wild to see. It's so exciting. I felt like I was witnessing history, like a scene in a movie. As we were walking through, didn't you have the feeling where you're like, oh this is way bigger than I thought?
turns.zh: 比手枪子弹快30倍，还有摩天大楼那么大。那太快了。看着太疯狂了。太令人兴奋了。我感觉自己在见证历史，就像电影里的场景。我们走过的时候，你有没有那种感觉，就是‘哦，这比我想象的大多了’？

turns.en: Gigantic.
turns.zh: 巨大。

turns.en: And the sheer numbers of them that you guys are making. And this is a version and you have a new updated version coming soon.
turns.zh: 而且你们制造的数量之多。这是一个版本，你们很快就会有新版本了。

turns.en: It's a little longer, same amount of pointy. The interstage section with the grill area is now integrated with the boost stage. We do what's called hot staging, where we light the ship engines while it's still attached to the booster. The booster engines are still thrusting, but then we light the ship engines and they pull away from the booster even though the booster engines are still firing. So it's blasting flame through that grill section, but we integrate that grill section into the boost stage with the next version of the rocket. The next version will have the Raptor 3 engines, which are a huge improvement. You may have seen them in the lobby—Raptor 1, 2, and 3. You can see the dramatic improvement in simplicity. We should put a plaque there to show how much we reduced weight, cost, and improved efficiency and thrust. Raptor 3 has almost twice the thrust of Raptor 1. It looks like it's got parts missing, very clean.
turns.zh: 它稍微长一点，尖度一样。带有格栅区域的级间段现在与助推段集成在一起。我们采用所谓的热分离，即在飞船仍连接在助推器上时点燃飞船发动机。助推器发动机仍在推进，但随后我们点燃飞船发动机，飞船发动机实际上会从助推器上脱离，尽管助推器发动机仍在燃烧。所以火焰会穿过那个格栅区域，但在下一版火箭中，我们将格栅区域集成到助推段中。下一版将配备猛禽3发动机，这是一个巨大的改进。你可能在大厅里见过——猛禽1、2和3。你可以看到在简化方面的巨大进步。我们应该在那里放一块牌匾，展示我们减轻了多少重量、降低了多少成本，以及提高了多少效率和推力。猛禽3的推力几乎是猛禽1的两倍。它看起来像是少了零件，非常干净。

turns.en: How many of them are on the rocket?
turns.zh: 火箭上有多少台？

turns.en: There are 33 on the booster. Each Raptor engine produces twice as much thrust as all four engines on a 747. So that engine is smaller than a 747 engine, but produces almost 10 times the thrust. Extremely high power-to-weight ratio.
turns.zh: 助推器上有33台。每台猛禽发动机产生的推力是747所有四台发动机的两倍。所以那台发动机比747的发动机小，但产生的推力几乎是其10倍。推重比极高。

turns.en: So when you're designing these, you get to Raptor 1, see its efficiency, see where you can improve, get to Raptor 2. How far can you scale this up with the same sort of technology?
turns.zh: 所以当你们设计这些时，从猛禽1开始，看到它的效率，看到可以改进的地方，再到猛禽2。用同样的技术，你们能把它扩展到什么程度？

turns.en: We're pushing the limits of physics here. To make a fully reusable orbital rocket, which no one has succeeded in doing yet, including us. But Starship is the first design where full and rapid reusability is actually possible. There hasn't even been a design before where it was possible, certainly not one that got any hardware. We live on a planet where gravity is quite high. If gravity were even 10 or 20% higher, we'd be stuck on Earth forever. We couldn't use conventional rockets; we'd have to blow ourselves off with a nuclear bomb or something crazy. But if Earth's gravity were just a little lower, even 10 or 20% lower, getting to orbit would be easy. It's like this is set to maximum difficulty but not impossible. Others have not ignored the concept of reusability; they just concluded it was too difficult. We've been working on this for a long time at SpaceX. I'm the chief engineer, but we have an extremely talented engineering team—the best rocket engineering team ever assembled.
turns.zh: 我们正在挑战物理极限。要制造一枚完全可重复使用的轨道火箭，还没有人成功过，包括我们。但星舰是第一个真正实现完全快速重复使用的设计。以前甚至没有这样的设计，更不用说造出硬件了。我们生活在一个重力相当高的星球上。如果重力再高10%或20%，我们就永远被困在地球上了。我们无法使用常规火箭，只能用核弹之类的东西把自己炸飞。但如果地球重力再低一点，哪怕低10%或20%，进入轨道就会很容易。这就像游戏设置了最高难度，但并非不可能。其他人并非忽视了可重复使用的概念，他们只是认为太难了。我们在SpaceX为此努力了很久。我是首席工程师，但我们有一个极其才华横溢的工程团队——有史以来最优秀的火箭工程团队。

turns.en: It's an honor to work with such incredible people. So it's fair to say that we have not yet succeeded in achieving full reusability, but we at last have a rocket where full reusability is possible. And I think we'll achieve it next year. That's a really big deal. The reason is that full reusability drops the cost of access to space by a hundred, maybe even more than a hundred. Could be like a thousand. You can think of it like any mode of transport. Imagine if aircraft were not reusable. You flew somewhere, you throw the plane away. Conventional rockets work like that: it would be like having an airplane, and instead of landing at your destination, you parachute out and the plane crashes. That would be a very expensive trip, and you'd need another plane to get back. But that's how other rockets work. The SpaceX Falcon rocket is the only one that is at least mostly reusable. You've seen the Falcon rocket land. We've now done over 500 landings of the Falcon 9 rocket. This year, we'll deliver probably somewhere between 2200 and 2500 tons to orbit with Falcon 9 and Falcon Heavy rockets, not counting anything from Starship.
turns.zh: 能与如此杰出的人共事是我的荣幸。可以说，我们尚未完全实现可重复使用，但我们终于有了一枚可以实现完全可重复使用的火箭。我认为我们明年就能实现。这意义重大。因为完全可重复使用能将进入太空的成本降低一百倍，甚至可能超过一百倍，也许能达到一千倍。你可以把它想象成任何一种交通工具。假设飞机不可重复使用，你飞到某地，然后把飞机扔掉。传统火箭就是这样运作的：就像你有一架飞机，但不在目的地降落，而是跳伞逃生，飞机坠毁。那样旅行会非常昂贵，而且你还得再找一架飞机回来。但这就是其他火箭的工作方式。SpaceX的猎鹰火箭是唯一至少大部分可重复使用的。你见过猎鹰火箭着陆。我们已经完成了超过500次猎鹰9号火箭的着陆。今年，我们将用猎鹰9号和猎鹰重型火箭向轨道运送大约2200到2500吨的载荷，这还不包括星舰的贡献。

turns.en: And this is mostly Starlink.
turns.zh: 这主要是星链吧？

turns.en: Yes, mostly Starlink, but we launch many others. We even launch our competitors to Starlink on Falcon 9. We charge them the same price. Pretty fair. SpaceX this year will deliver roughly 90% of all Earth mass to orbit.
turns.zh: 是的，主要是星链，但我们也发射很多其他载荷。我们甚至用猎鹰9号发射星链的竞争对手。我们向他们收取同样的价格。相当公平。SpaceX今年将把地球上大约90%的质量送入轨道。

turns.en: Wow.
turns.zh: 哇。

turns.en: And of the remaining 10%, most of that is done by China. And then the remaining roughly 4% is everyone else in the world, including our domestic competitors.
turns.zh: 剩下的10%中，大部分由中国完成。然后剩下的约4%由世界其他地区完成，包括我们国内的竞争对手。

turns.en: It's incredible how many things are in space, floating above us now.
turns.zh: 太不可思议了，现在太空中有那么多东西漂浮在我们头顶。

turns.en: There's a lot of things.
turns.zh: 确实有很多东西。

turns.en: Is there a saturation point where we'll have problems with all these satellites?
turns.zh: 有没有一个饱和点，到时候所有这些卫星会带来问题？

turns.en: I think as long as the satellites are maintained, it'll be fine. Space is very roomy. You can think of space as concentric shells of the surface of the Earth. It's a series of concentric trails. There's a lot of room. Imagine if there were just a few thousand Airstreams on Earth. What are the odds they'd hit each other? They wouldn't be very crowded. And you go higher, hundreds of miles above Earth.
turns.zh: 我认为只要卫星得到维护，就没问题。太空非常宽敞。你可以把太空想象成地球表面的同心壳层。它是一系列同心轨道。空间很大。想象一下地球上只有几千辆房车。它们相撞的概率有多大？不会很拥挤。而且你还可以去更高的地方，距离地球数百英里。

turns.en: The goal of SpaceX is to get rocket technology to the point where we can extend life beyond Earth and establish a self-sustaining city on Mars, and a permanent base on the moon. That would be very cool. Imagine if we had a moon base alpha with a permanent science base on the moon.
turns.zh: SpaceX的目标是将火箭技术发展到能够将生命扩展到地球之外，在火星上建立一个自给自足的城市，并在月球上建立一个永久基地。那将非常酷。想象一下，如果我们在月球上有一个阿尔法基地，一个永久性的科学基地。

turns.en: That would be pretty dope. Or at least a tourist trap.
turns.zh: 那太棒了。或者至少是个旅游胜地。

turns.en: A lot of people would be willing to go to the moon just for a tour. That's for sure. We could probably pay for our space program with that.
turns.zh: 很多人会愿意去月球旅游。这是肯定的。我们也许可以用这个来为我们的太空计划买单。

turns.en: Probably. If you could go to the moon safely, I think a lot of people would pay for that.
turns.zh: 很可能。如果能安全地去月球，我想很多人会为此付费。

turns.en: Oh, 100%. After the first year, after nobody died.
turns.zh: 哦，100%。第一年之后，如果没有人死亡。

turns.en: Exactly. Are you going to come back?
turns.zh: 没错。你会回来吗？

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Like that submarine, they had a bunch of successful launches before it imploded and killed everybody. That was a very bad design.
turns.zh: 就像那艘潜艇，在它内爆并导致所有人遇难之前，他们有过多次成功发射。那是一个非常糟糕的设计。

turns.en: Terrible design. And the engineers said it would not withstand the pressure of those depths. There were a lot of whistleblowers in that company. They made it out of carbon fiber, which doesn't make any sense because you need to be dense to go down. Just make it out of steel. If you make it out of a big steel casting, you'll be safe. Why would they make it out of carbon fiber? Is it cheaper?
turns.zh: 糟糕的设计。工程师们说它无法承受那些深度的压力。那家公司有很多举报人。他们用碳纤维制造，这毫无道理，因为你需要密度大才能下潜。直接用钢造就行了。如果用大型钢铸件，你会很安全。他们为什么用碳纤维？是因为便宜吗？

turns.en: I think they think carbon fiber sounds cool.
turns.zh: 我想他们觉得碳纤维听起来很酷。

turns.en: It does sound cool, but because it's such low density, you actually have to add extra mass to go down. But if you just have a giant hollow ball bearing, you'll be fine.
turns.zh: 听起来确实很酷，但由于密度很低，你实际上需要增加额外质量才能下潜。但如果你有一个巨大的空心滚珠轴承，就没问题。

turns.en: Speaking of carbon fiber, did you check out my unplugged Tesla out there? Pretty sick, right? Have you guys ever thought about doing something like that, like having an AMG division of Tesla where you do custom stuff?
turns.zh: 说到碳纤维，你看到我那辆改装的Tesla了吗？很酷吧？你们有没有想过做类似的事情，比如像AMG那样搞一个Tesla的定制部门？

turns.en: I think it's best to leave that to the custom shops. Tesla's focus is autonomous cars, building kind of futuristic autonomous cars. We want the future to look like the future. Did you see our designs for the robotic bus? It looks pretty cool. It's very art deco, futuristic art deco. We want to change the aesthetic over time. You don't want the aesthetic to be constant. You want to evolve it. I have a son, Saxon, who is even more autistic than me, and he has these great observations because he views the world through a different lens. He said, 'Dad, why does the world look like it's 2015?' And I thought, damn, the world does look like it's 2015. The aesthetic has not evolved since 2015.
turns.zh: 我认为最好留给定制店去做。Tesla的焦点是自动驾驶汽车，打造未来主义的自动驾驶汽车。我们希望未来看起来像未来。你看到我们设计的机器人巴士了吗？它看起来非常酷。它是装饰艺术风格，未来主义的装饰艺术。我们希望随着时间的推移改变美学。你不希望美学一成不变。你想让它进化。我有一个儿子叫Saxon，他比我还自闭，但他有很棒的观察力，因为他用不同的视角看世界。他说：‘爸爸，为什么世界看起来像2015年？’我想，该死，世界确实看起来像2015年。美学自2015年以来就没有进化过。

turns.en: Oh, that's what it looks like.
turns.zh: 哦，原来是这样。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: That's pretty cool. Oh, yeah. That's like you'd want to see that going down the road, you know? Yeah. You'd be like, 'Okay, this is we're in the future.' You know, it doesn't look like 2015. What is that ancient science fiction movie? Like one of the first science fiction movies ever. Is it Metropolis? Is that what it is? Yeah. Yeah. That looks like it belongs in Metropolis. Yeah. It's a futuristic art deco. All right. Yeah. Well, that's cool that you're concentrating on the aesthetic. I mean, that's kind of the whole deal with Cybertruck, right? Like, it didn't have to look like that.
turns.zh: 那太酷了。哦，是啊。就像你希望看到它行驶在路上，你知道吗？是啊。你会觉得，‘好吧，这就是未来。’你知道，它看起来不像2015年。那部古老的科幻电影是什么？最早期的科幻电影之一。是《大都会》吗？是那个吗？是啊。是啊。它看起来就像属于《大都会》。是啊。未来主义装饰艺术风格。好吧。是啊。你专注于美学，这很酷。我的意思是，这基本上就是Cybertruck的全部意义，对吧？它本来不必长那样。

turns.en: No, I just wanted to have something that looked really different.
turns.zh: 不，我只是想要一些看起来非常不同的东西。

turns.en: Is it a pain in the ass for people to get it insured because it's all solid steel?
turns.zh: 人们给它上保险是不是很麻烦，因为它全是实心钢？

turns.en: I hope it's not too much. Tesla does offer insurance, so people can always get it insured at Tesla.
turns.zh: 我希望不会太贵。特斯拉确实提供保险，所以人们总可以在特斯拉投保。

turns.en: Well, but the form does follow function in the case of the Cybertruck because as you demonstrated with your armor-piercing arrow, if you shot that arrow at a regular truck, you would have found your arrow in the wall. At the very least, it would have buried into one of the seats.
turns.zh: 嗯，但Cybertruck确实是形式服从功能，因为你用穿甲箭演示过，如果你朝普通卡车射那支箭，你会发现箭在墙上。至少，它会嵌进一个座位里。

turns.en: Exactly. You could definitely get enough bow velocity and the right arrow would go through both doors of a regular truck and land on the wall. If there was a clear shot between both doors, it probably would have passed right through. The arrow shattered on the Cybertruck because it's ultra-hard stainless steel. I thought it'd be cool to have a truck that is bulletproof to subsonic projectiles. Especially in this day and age, if the apocalypse happens, you're going to want to have a bulletproof truck. Because it's made of ultra-hard stainless, you can't stamp the panels. You can't put it in a stamping press because it breaks the press. So it has to be planar because it's so difficult to bend it. That's why it's so boxy, because it's bulletproof steel.
turns.zh: 没错。你肯定能获得足够的弓速，合适的箭会穿过普通卡车的两扇门，然后落在墙上。如果两扇门之间没有遮挡，它很可能直接穿过去。箭在Cybertruck上碎裂了，因为它是超硬不锈钢。我觉得有一辆能防亚音速子弹的卡车会很酷。尤其是在这个时代，如果世界末日来了，你会想要一辆防弹卡车。因为它由超硬不锈钢制成，你不能冲压面板。你不能把它放进冲压机，因为会损坏机器。所以它必须是平面的，因为太难弯曲了。这就是为什么它这么方正，因为它是防弹钢。

turns.en: So it is boxy as opposed to curved.
turns.zh: 所以它是方方正正的，而不是流线型。

turns.en: Yeah. To make curved shapes, you take mild steel, put it in a stamping press, and it smooshes it into whatever shape you want. But the Cybertruck is made of ultra-hard stainless, so you can't stamp it because it would break the press. Even bending it is hard. We have to way overbend it so that when it springs back, it's in the right position. I think it's a unique aesthetic. What's cool about a truck? Trucks should be manly, macho, and bulletproof is maximum macho.
turns.zh: 是的。要制造弯曲形状，你用软钢，放进冲压机，它就能压成你想要的任何形状。但Cybertruck用的是超硬不锈钢，所以不能冲压，因为会损坏机器。连弯曲它都很难。我们必须过度弯曲，这样它回弹时才能处于正确位置。我认为这是一种独特的美学。卡车酷在哪里？卡车应该阳刚、硬汉，而防弹是最硬汉的。

turns.en: Are you married to that shape now? Can you do anything to change it? Like you updated the Model 3 and Model Y.
turns.zh: 你现在是坚持那个形状了吗？你能做些什么来改变它吗？就像你更新了Model 3和Model Y。

turns.en: Yes, the Model 3 and Model Y are updated. There's a screen in the back for the kids to watch, for example, in the new 3 and Y. In the new Y, there are hundreds of improvements. We keep improving the car. Even the Cybertruck, we keep improving it. But I wanted to do something that looked unique, and the Cybertruck looks unique and has unique functionality. There were three things: make it bulletproof, make it faster than a Porsche 911, and we actually cleared the quarter mile. The Cybertruck can clear a quarter mile while towing a Porsche 911 faster than a Porsche 911. It can out-tow an F350 diesel.
turns.zh: 是的，Model 3和Model Y已经更新了。例如，在新款3和Y中，后座有一个屏幕供孩子们观看。在新款Y中，有数百项改进。我们不断改进汽车。即使是Cybertruck，我们也在不断改进。但我想做一些看起来独特的东西，Cybertruck看起来独特，功能也独特。有三件事：让它防弹，让它比保时捷911更快，而且我们确实跑完了四分之一英里。Cybertruck在拖着一辆保时捷911的情况下，跑完四分之一英里比保时捷911本身还快。它的牵引力超过F350柴油车。

turns.en: Really? What are the tow limitations?
turns.zh: 真的吗？牵引限制是多少？

turns.en: I mean, we could tow a 747 with it. The Cybertruck is insanely alien technology. It shouldn't be possible to be that big and that fast. It's like an elephant that runs like a cheetah.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我们可以用它拖一架747。Cybertruck是疯狂的外星技术。不可能这么大还这么快。就像一头跑得像猎豹一样快的大象。

turns.en: Yeah, because it's 0 to 60 in less than 3 seconds, right? And it's enormous. What does it weigh? Like 7,000 lbs.
turns.zh: 是啊，因为它0到60英里加速不到3秒，对吧？而且它很大。它有多重？大概7000磅。

turns.en: Yeah, there are different configurations, but it's about that. It's a beast. It has four-wheel steering, so the rear wheels steer too. It has a very tight turning radius.
turns.zh: 是的，有不同的配置，但差不多。它是个猛兽。它有四轮转向，所以后轮也能转向。转弯半径非常小。

turns.en: Yeah, we noticed that when we drove one to Starbase. Very tight turning radius. Pretty sick.
turns.zh: 是啊，我们开了一辆去星舰基地时就注意到了。转弯半径非常小。太酷了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Are you still doing the Roadster?
turns.zh: 你还在做Roadster吗？

turns.en: Yes. Eventually, we're getting close to demonstrating the prototype, and I think this will be... one thing I can guarantee is that this product demo will be unforgettable. Unforgettable.
turns.zh: 是的。最终，我们接近展示原型了，我认为这将是……我能保证的一件事是，这次产品演示将令人难忘。令人难忘。

turns.en: How so? Whether it's good or bad, it will be unforgettable. Can you say more? What do you mean?
turns.zh: 怎么说？无论好坏，都会令人难忘。你能多说点吗？你什么意思？

turns.en: Well, my friend Peter Thiel once reflected that the future was supposed to have flying cars, but we don't have flying cars.
turns.zh: 嗯，我的朋友彼得·蒂尔曾反思说，未来本应有飞行汽车，但我们没有飞行汽车。

turns.en: So you're going to be able to fly? I mean, I think if Peter wants a flying car, we should be able to buy one.
turns.zh: 所以你将能够飞行？我的意思是，我认为如果彼得想要一辆飞行汽车，我们应该能买到一辆。

turns.en: Well, we have to see in the demo.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们得在演示中看看。

turns.en: So when you do this, are you going to have a retractable wing? Like, what is the idea behind this? Don't be sly. Come on.
turns.zh: 那么当你做这个的时候，你会有一个可伸缩的机翼吗？比如，这背后的想法是什么？别耍滑头。说吧。

turns.en: I can't do the unveil before the unveil.
turns.zh: 我不能在发布之前就发布。

turns.en: Tell me off air then.
turns.zh: 那私下告诉我。

turns.en: I think it has a shot at being the most memorable product unveil ever. It has a shot.
turns.zh: 我认为它有可能成为有史以来最令人难忘的产品发布。有可能。

turns.en: And when do you plan on doing this? What's the goal?
turns.zh: 你计划什么时候做这个？目标是什么？

turns.en: Hopefully before the end of the year.
turns.zh: 希望在今年年底之前。

turns.en: Really? Before the end of this year? This is... I mean we're in a couple months.
turns.zh: 真的吗？今年年底之前？这……我是说我们还有几个月。

turns.en: Hopefully in a couple months. We need to make sure it works. This is some crazy technology we got in this car. Crazy technology. So different from what was previously announced.
turns.zh: 希望几个月后吧。我们得确保它管用。这车里有疯狂的技术，跟之前宣布的完全不同。

turns.en: Yes. And is that why you haven't released it yet? Because you keep working on it?
turns.zh: 是的。所以你还没发布是因为一直在改进它？

turns.en: It has crazy technology. Is it even a car? I'm not sure. It looks like a car, but it's crazier than anything James Bond. If you took all the James Bond cars and combined them, it's crazier than that.
turns.zh: 它有疯狂的技术。它还算车吗？我不确定。它看起来像车，但比所有詹姆斯·邦德的车加起来还疯狂。

turns.en: Very exciting. I don't know what to think of that.
turns.zh: 太令人兴奋了。我不知道该怎么想。

turns.en: I don't know.
turns.zh: 我不知道。

turns.en: It's a limited amount of information I'm drawing from here. Jamie's very suspicious over there. Look at him. Excited. I'm interested.
turns.zh: 我掌握的信息有限。杰米那边很怀疑，你看他。兴奋，我感兴趣。

turns.en: It's still going to be the same.
turns.zh: 它还是会一样的。

turns.en: Well, if you want to come a little before the unveil, I can show it to you 100%. Yeah, let's go.
turns.zh: 如果你愿意在发布前提前一点来，我可以百分之百展示给你看。好，走吧。

turns.en: It's kind of crazy all the different things you're involved in simultaneously. We talked about this before, your time management, but I really don't understand it. I don't understand how you can be paying attention to all these different things simultaneously: Starlink, SpaceX, Tesla, Boring Company, X. You tweet or post rather all day long.
turns.zh: 你同时参与这么多不同的事情，真是疯狂。我们之前聊过你的时间管理，但我真的不理解。我不明白你怎么能同时关注所有这些不同的事情：星链、SpaceX、特斯拉、无聊公司、X。你整天发推文或者说发帖。

turns.en: It's more like I can hop in for like two minutes and then hop out.
turns.zh: 更像是，我可以跳进去两分钟，然后跳出来。

turns.en: But I mean, just the fact that you could do...
turns.zh: 但我的意思是，你能做到这一点……

turns.en: Bathroom break or whatever.
turns.zh: 就像上厕所之类的。

turns.en: I can't do that. If I hop in, I start scrolling and looking around. Next thing you know, I've lost an hour.
turns.zh: 我做不到。我一跳进去就开始刷屏，四处看，转眼就浪费了一个小时。

turns.en: For me it's a couple minutes usually. Sometimes it's half an hour, but usually I'm in for a few minutes then out, posting something on X. I do sometimes feel like that meme of the guy who drops the grenade and leaves the room. That's been me more than once on X.
turns.zh: 对我来说通常就几分钟。有时候半小时，但通常我进去几分钟就出来，在X上发点东西。我有时候觉得自己就像那个扔了手榴弹就离开房间的表情包。我在X上不止一次这样。

turns.en: It's got to be fun, though, to know that you essentially disrupted the entire social media chain of command. There was a very clear thing going on with social media. The government had infiltrated it, they were censoring speech, and until you bought it, we really didn't know the extent of it. We kind of assumed something was going on.
turns.zh: 不过，知道你基本上颠覆了整个社交媒体指挥链，一定很有趣。社交媒体上发生着很明显的事情。政府渗透了它，他们在审查言论，直到你买下它，我们才真正知道程度有多深。我们之前只是猜测有些不对劲。

turns.en: We had no idea that they were actively involved in censoring actual real news stories, real data, real scientists, real professors silenced, expelled, kicked off the platform. For telling the truth.
turns.zh: 我们不知道他们积极参与审查真实的新闻故事、真实的数据、真实的科学家、真实的教授被噤声、开除、赶出平台。就因为他们说了真话。

turns.en: Wild. For telling the truth. And I'm sure you've also seen that chart that shows young kids, teenagers identifying as trans and non-binary literally stops dead when you bought Twitter and starts falling off a cliff when people are allowed to have rational discussions now and actually talk about it.
turns.zh: 太疯狂了。就因为说了真话。我相信你也看到了那张图表，显示认同跨性别和非二元性别的青少年在你买下推特时戛然而止，当人们被允许进行理性讨论并真正谈论它时，开始断崖式下跌。

turns.en: Yes. I said at the time that the reason for acquiring Twitter was because it was causing destruction at a civilizational level. I posted on Twitter at the time that it is Wormtongue for the world, like Wormtongue from Lord of the Rings who would whisper terrible things to the king so the king would believe things that weren't true. Unfortunately, Twitter really got controlled by the woke mob, and they were pushing a nihilistic anti-civilizational mind virus to the world. You can see the results of that mind virus on the streets of San Francisco, where downtown San Francisco looks like a zombie apocalypse. It's bad. We don't want the whole world to be a zombie apocalypse. They were pushing this very negative, nihilistic, untrue worldview on the world and it was causing a lot of damage.
turns.zh: 是的。我当时说过，收购推特的原因是它在文明层面造成了破坏。我当时在推特上发帖说，它是世界的‘巧言令色者’，就像《指环王》里的巧言令色者，对国王低语可怕的事情，让国王相信不真实的事情。不幸的是，推特真的被觉醒暴民控制了，他们在向世界推行一种虚无主义、反文明的思维病毒。你可以在旧金山的街头看到这种思维病毒的结果，市中心看起来像僵尸末日。这很糟糕。我们不希望整个世界都变成僵尸末日。他们在向世界推行这种非常消极、虚无主义、不真实的世界观，造成了很大的损害。

turns.en: The stunning thing about it is how few people course corrected. A bunch of people woke up and realized what was going on. People that were all on board with woke ideology in maybe 2015 or 16 and then eventually it comes to affect them or they see it in their workplace and they're like, 'Whoa, whoa, whoa, we got to stop this.' A bunch of people did, but a lot of people never course corrected.
turns.zh: 令人震惊的是，很少有人纠正方向。一群人醒悟过来，意识到发生了什么。那些在2015或2016年可能都支持觉醒意识形态的人，最终当它影响到他们或他们在工作场所看到时，他们会说，‘哇，哇，哇，我们必须阻止这个。’很多人确实这样做了，但很多人从未纠正方向。

turns.en: A lot of people didn't course correct, but it's gone directionally correct. Like you mentioned, the massive spike in kids identifying as trans and then that spike dropping after the Twitter acquisition. I think that simply allowing the truth to be told was shedding sunlight. Sunlight is the best disinfectant, as they say, and just allowing sunlight kills the virus.
turns.zh: 很多人没有纠正方向，但方向上是正确的。就像你提到的，认同跨性别的孩子数量激增，然后在收购推特后下降。我认为，仅仅允许真相被说出来，就是让阳光照进来。俗话说，阳光是最好的消毒剂，让阳光照进来就能杀死病毒。

turns.en: And it also changed the benchmark for all the other platforms. You can't just openly censor people on all the other platforms because X is available. So Facebook announced they were changing, YouTube announced they were changing their policies, and they're kind of forced to. And then BlueSky doubled down.
turns.zh: 而且它也改变了所有其他平台的基准。你不能在其他平台上公开审查人们，因为X存在。所以Facebook宣布他们正在改变，YouTube宣布他们正在改变政策，他们有点被迫这样做。然后BlueSky加倍坚持。

turns.en: The problem is that the woke mind virus retreated to BlueSky. But it's just a self-reinforcing lunatic asylum.
turns.zh: 问题是，觉醒思维病毒撤退到了BlueSky。但那只是一个自我强化的疯人院。

turns.en: They're all just triple masked. I was watching this exchange on BlueSky where someone said they're just trying to be zen about something, and then a moderator immediately chimed in, 'Why don't you try to stop being racist against Asians by saying something zen?' They were accusing that person of being racist towards Asians.
turns.zh: 他们都戴着三重面具。我在BlueSky上看到一段对话，有人说他们只是想对某事保持禅意，然后一个版主立即插话说，‘你为什么不说点禅意的话来停止对亚洲人的种族歧视？’他们指责那个人对亚洲人种族歧视。

turns.en: It's just everyone's a hall monitor over there. The worst hall monitor. A virgin incel.
turns.zh: 那边每个人都是走廊监督员。最糟糕的走廊监督员。一个处男非自愿独身者。

turns.en: They're all hall monitors trying to rat on each other. It's fascinating. And then people say, 'I'm leaving for BlueSky like Stephen King.' And a couple weeks later, he's back on X. Just like, 'Fuck it. There's no one over there. It's all a bunch of crazy people. You can only stay in the asylum for so long.' They all bail. Threads is kind of like that, too.
turns.zh: 他们都是走廊监督员，互相告密。真有趣。然后人们说，‘我要像斯蒂芬·金一样离开去BlueSky。’几周后，他又回到X了。就像，‘去他的。那边没人。全是一群疯子。你在疯人院只能待那么久。’他们都逃了。Threads也差不多。

turns.en: Threads is... I've been on Threads. What happens is if you go on Instagram, every now and then something really stupid will pop up on Threads, and it shows it to you on Instagram. Then I'll click on that and go to Threads, and you see posts with like 25 likes, famous people with like 50. It's down. But the people that post there find that there's very little pushback from insane ideology, so they go there and spit out nonsense, and very few people jump in to argue.
turns.zh: Threads 这个平台……我上过 Threads。情况是，如果你刷 Instagram，时不时会看到 Threads 上冒出来特别蠢的东西，Instagram 会推给你。然后我点进去，到了 Threads，看到的帖子只有 25 个赞，名人也就 50 个。热度很低。但发帖的人发现，那些疯狂的观点几乎没人反驳，所以他们就在那里胡说八道，很少有人跳出来争论。
对，很奇怪，非常奇怪的地方。
turns.en: Yeah, very weird, very weird place.

I mean, I can generally get the vibe of what's taking off by seeing what's showing up on X, because that's the public town square still. And also what links show up in group texts. If I'm in a group chat with friends, what links are showing up? That's what I try to do now. Only get stuff that shows up in my group text because that keeps me productive. So I only check if someone's like, 'Dude, what the fuck?' like, 'All right, let me check it out.' If there's something crazy enough that it ends up in the group chat, but there's always something. That's what's nuts. There's always some new law that's passed, some new insane thing that California is doing. A giant chunk of it is happening in California. The most preposterous things that I get.
turns.zh: 我是说，我大致能通过 X 上出现的内容感受到什么在流行，因为 X 仍然是公共广场。还有群聊里分享的链接。如果我在朋友群聊里，大家分享什么链接？我现在就只看群聊里出现的东西，这样能保持高效。所以只有当有人说‘老兄，什么鬼？’的时候，我才会说‘好吧，让我看看’。如果有足够疯狂的事情出现在群聊里，但总会有事情。这就是疯狂的地方。总有一些新法律通过，一些加州做的疯狂事。很大一部分都发生在加州。我收到的最荒谬的事情。
对。
turns.en: Yeah.

And then you got Gavin Newsom running around saying we all have California derangement syndrome. He's just ripping off Trump derangement and calling it California derangement. I was like, 'No, no, no. How many corporations have left California?'
turns.zh: 然后加文·纽森到处说我们都有‘加州妄想症’。他就是在剽窃‘特朗普妄想症’的说法，改成‘加州妄想症’。我说，‘不不不，有多少公司已经离开加州了？’
疯了。
turns.en: It's crazy.

turns.zh: 几百家。
turns.en: Hundreds.

turns.zh: 几百家，对吧？
turns.en: Hundreds, right?

turns.zh: 几百家。这不好。
turns.en: Hundreds. That's not good.

turns.zh: 我是说，不是 Chick-fil-A。我是说，我想 In-N-Out 离开了。
turns.en: I mean, not Chick-fil-A. I mean, I think In-N-Out left.

turns.zh: 对，In-N-Out 搬到了田纳西。
turns.en: Yeah. In-N-Out moved to Tennessee.

turns.zh: 对。
turns.en: Yeah.

turns.zh: 他们就像，‘我们再也受不了了。’
turns.en: They're like, 'We can't do this anymore.'

turns.zh: 对。
turns.en: Right.

turns.zh: 而且它是加州的食品公司，是最好的汉堡店。
turns.en: And it's the California company for food. It's like the greatest hamburger place ever.

turns.zh: 太棒了。
turns.en: It's awesome.

turns.zh: 对。实际上，说到开源和公开看待事物，我喜欢走进 In-N-Out，看着他们做汉堡。
turns.en: Yeah. Actually, speaking of open source and looking at things openly, I like going into In-N-Out and seeing them make the burger.

turns.zh: 对，就在你面前。
turns.en: Yeah. It's right there.

turns.zh: 他们切洋葱，你看着所有东西在你面前做出来。
turns.en: They chop the onions, and you see everything getting made in front of you.

turns.zh: 对，很棒。
turns.en: Yeah. It's great.

turns.zh: 但是，你需要多少次警钟才能说加州需要改革？
turns.en: But yeah, like, how many wake-up calls do you need to say that there needs to be reform in California?

turns.zh: 嗯，纽森做的疯狂事是，每当有人提起加州的问题，他就开始列举所有积极面：最多的财富 500 强公司，最高的教育水平。但那些在你当州长之前就已经存在了。
turns.en: Well, the crazy thing that Newsom does is whenever someone brings up the problems in California, he starts rattling off all the positives: the most Fortune 500 companies, highest education. But that was all already there before you were governor.

turns.zh: 但是有多少财富 500 强公司离开了加州？
turns.en: But how many Fortune 500 companies have left California?

turns.zh: 然后你们花了 240 亿美元在无家可归者问题上，情况却变得更糟了。
turns.en: And then you guys spent $24 billion on the homeless and it got way worse.

turns.zh: 对，无家可归者人口翻了一番之类的。但人们不理解无家可归问题，因为它利用了人们的同情心。我认为我们应该有同情心，应该努力帮助别人。但无家可归者产业综合体真的很黑暗。那个 NGO 网络应该被称为‘药物僵尸养殖者’。因为无家可归者越多，当你遇到一个完全麻木、拖着腿在路上走、腿上还挂着针头的人时，‘无家可归’这个词用错了。‘无家可归’暗示某人房贷稍微逾期，如果得到一份工作就能重新站起来。但那些吸食芬太尼、在街上拉屎、身上有开放性疮口的人，不是一份工作就能解决的。这不是无家可归问题。‘无家可归’是一个宣传词汇。这些慈善机构根据无家可归者或药物僵尸的数量获得资金。所以他们的激励机制是最大化药物僵尸的数量，而不是最小化。这就是为什么他们不逮捕毒贩，因为如果逮捕毒贩，药物僵尸就会离开。他们知道毒贩是谁，故意不逮捕。否则药物僵尸会离开，他们就会停止从加州和所有慈善机构获得资金。
turns.en: Yes. The homeless population doubled or something. But people don't understand the homeless thing because it preys on people's empathy. I think we should have empathy and try to help people. But the homeless industrial complex is really dark. That network of NGOs should be called 'drug zombie farmers.' Because the more homeless people, and when you meet someone who's totally dead inside, shuffling along the street with a needle dangling out of their leg, 'homeless' is the wrong word. 'Homeless' implies someone got a little behind on their mortgage and if they got a job offer, they'd be back on their feet. But someone who's on fentanyl, taking a dump in the middle of the street, with open sores, they're not one job offer away from getting back on their feet. This is not a homeless issue. 'Homeless' is a propaganda word. These charities get money proportionate to the number of homeless people or drug zombies. So their incentive structure is to maximize the number of drug zombies, not minimize it. That's why they don't arrest the drug dealers, because if they arrest the drug dealers, the drug zombies leave. They know who the drug dealers are and don't arrest them on purpose, because otherwise the drug zombies would leave and they would stop getting money from the state of California and from all the charities.

turns.zh: 等等，所以他们和执法部门协调？
turns.en: Wait a minute. So they're in coordination with law enforcement on this?

turns.zh: 对。
turns.en: Yeah.

turns.zh: 那他们怎么开会？
turns.en: So how do they have those meetings?

turns.zh: 他们都是一伙的。这是一个恶魔般的骗局。而且旧金山有这个总收入税，不是对收入征税，而是对交易征税。这就是为什么 Stripe、Square 和一大堆金融公司不得不搬离旧金山，因为不是对收入征税，而是对交易征税。如果你做了数万亿美元的交易，那不是收入。在旧金山，任何经过系统的钱都要被征税。杰克·多西指出了这一点，他说 Square 不得不从旧金山搬到奥克兰，Stripe 不得不从旧金山搬到南旧金山，不同的城市。那笔钱流向了无家可归者产业综合体，就是通过那个税。每年有数十亿美元流向这些由国家资助的非政府组织。不清楚如何关闭它。这是一个自我循环的冰淇淋蛋筒情况。他们得到的钱与无家可归者或药物僵尸的数量成正比。所以他们试图增加这个数量。在某些情况下，有人分析过，把所有流出的钱加起来，每个无家可归者或药物僵尸接近一百万美元。
turns.en: They're all in cahoots. It's a diabolical scam. And San Francisco has this gross receipts tax, which is not on revenue, it's on transactions. That's why Stripe, Square, and a whole bunch of financial companies had to move out of San Francisco, because it wasn't a tax on revenue, it's taxed on transactions. If you did trillions of dollars in transactions, that's not revenue. You're taxed on any money going through the system in San Francisco. Jack Dorsey pointed this out, and they said Square had to move from San Francisco to Oakland, Stripe had to move from San Francisco to South San Francisco, a different city. That money goes to the homeless industrial complex through that tax. So there are billions of dollars every year that go to these non-governmental organizations funded by the state. It's not clear how to turn this off. It's a self-licking ice cream cone situation. They get this money proportionate to the number of homeless people or drug zombies. So they try to increase that number. In some cases, somebody did an analysis: when you add up all the money flowing, they're getting close to a million dollars per homeless person or drug zombie.

It's like $900,000 or something, some crazy amount of money is going to these organizations. So they want to keep people just barely alive. They need to keep them in the area so they get the revenue. That's why they don't arrest the drug dealers, because otherwise the drug zombies would leave. But they don't want them to have too much drugs, or they die. So they're kept in this perpetual zone of being addicted but just barely alive.
turns.zh: 这就像90万美元之类的，一大笔钱流向了这些组织。所以他们想让人们勉强活着。他们需要把他们留在那个区域，这样他们才能获得收入。这就是为什么他们不逮捕毒贩，否则那些吸毒成瘾的僵尸就会离开。但他们也不想让他们有太多毒品，否则他们会死。所以他们被维持在这种成瘾但勉强活着的永久状态。

turns.en: So how is this coordinated with DAs that don't prosecute people? When they hire or push, they fund the campaigns of the most progressive, most out there leftwing DAs, they get them into office.
turns.zh: 那么这与那些不起诉人的地方检察官是如何协调的呢？当他们雇佣或推动时，他们资助最进步、最极端的左翼地方检察官的竞选活动，让他们上台。

turns.zh: 你看到那个在图书馆被枪杀的家伙了吗？

turns.zh: 没有。

turns.zh: 是的，我听说有个家伙在图书馆被枪杀了。我想那大概是上周的事，对吧？

turns.en: Oh Jesus.
turns.zh: 哦，天哪。

turns.en: And that's when someone got shot.
turns.zh: 然后有人就被枪杀了。

turns.en: Yeah, I believe this should be on the news. You might be able to pull it up. But I think it was just in the last week or so that there was a shooting in the library in Austin. Because Austin's got, you know, it's the most liberal part of Texas that we're in right here.
turns.zh: 是的，我相信这应该上新闻了。你可能能查到。但我认为就在上周左右，奥斯汀的图书馆发生了一起枪击案。因为奥斯汀，你知道，是我们现在所在的德克萨斯州最自由的部分。

turns.en: So suspect involved in the shooting at Austin Park Library Saturday is accused of another shooting at the Cap Metro bus earlier that day. According to an arrest warrant affidavit, Austin police arrested Harold Newton Keen, 55, shortly after the shooting at the library, which occurred around noon. One person sustained non-life-threatening injuries. Before that shooting, Keen was accused of shooting another person in a bus incident and after reportedly pointing his gun at a child. So, this is the fella down here.
turns.zh: 所以周六奥斯汀公园图书馆枪击案的嫌疑人还被指控当天早些时候在Cap Metro公交车上犯下另一起枪击案。根据逮捕令宣誓书，奥斯汀警方在中午左右发生的图书馆枪击案后不久逮捕了55岁的Harold Newton Keen。一人受轻伤，无生命危险。在那次枪击之前，Keen被指控在公交事件中枪击另一人，随后据报道还用枪指着一名儿童。所以，这就是那个家伙。

turns.en: So, we just have a serious problem here.
turns.zh: 所以，我们这里确实有个严重的问题。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: You know, so I think one of the people might have died too that he shot. I think one of them did bleed out.
turns.zh: 你知道，所以我认为他枪击的人中可能有一个也死了。我想有一个人确实流血过多死了。

turns.en: But either way, getting shot is still bad. It says the victim told police he confronted the suspect who started to eat what appeared to be crystal methamphetamine. According to the affidavit, the victim advised the suspect began to trip out, at which time the victim exited the bus. The victim told the bus driver to hit the panic button and then exited the bus. When he turned around, he observed a black male now standing at the front of the bus with the gun pointed at him. The victim advised the black male fired a single round which grazed his left hip. So he shot at that dude and then another dude got shot in the library. Fun.
turns.zh: 但不管怎样，被枪击还是很糟糕。报道说受害者告诉警察，他质问嫌疑人，嫌疑人开始吃看起来像冰毒的东西。根据宣誓书，受害者说嫌疑人开始出现幻觉，于是受害者下了公交车。受害者告诉公交车司机按下紧急按钮，然后下了车。当他转身时，看到一个黑人男子站在公交车前部，用枪指着他。受害者说那个黑人男子开了一枪，擦伤了他的左臀部。所以他朝那个家伙开了枪，然后另一个家伙在图书馆被枪杀了。真有意思。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean in the library.
turns.zh: 是啊，在图书馆里。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: You know, where you're supposed to be reading books. And there's a children's section in the library and he pointed his gun at a kid. I mean, we do have a serious issue in America where repeat violent offenders need to be incarcerated, right?
turns.zh: 你知道，那是个本该读书的地方。图书馆里还有儿童区，他却用枪指着孩子。我的意思是，美国确实有个严重问题，那就是重复暴力犯罪者需要被监禁，对吧？

turns.en: And you got cases where somebody's been arrested like 47 times, right? Like literally. That's just the number of times they were arrested, not the number of times they did things. Most of the times they do things, they're not arrested.
turns.zh: 而且有些案子，有人被捕了47次，对吧？就是字面意义上的。那只是他们被捕的次数，不是他们做坏事的次数。大多数时候他们做了坏事，根本没被捕。

turns.en: So lay this out for people so they understand how this happens.
turns.zh: 所以给人们解释一下，让他们明白这是怎么发生的。

turns.en: Yeah. And the key is this: it preys on people's empathy. If you're a good person, you want good things to happen in the world. You're like, well, we should take care of people who are down on their luck or having a hard time in life. And I agree. But what we shouldn't do is put people who are violent drug zombies in public places where they can hurt other people. And that's what we're doing. We just saw a guy got shot in the library, but even before that he shot another guy and pointed his gun at a kid. That guy probably has many prior arrests.
turns.zh: 是的。关键是这样的：它利用了人们的同情心。如果你是个好人，你希望世界上发生好事。你会说，我们应该照顾那些运气不好或生活艰难的人。我同意。但我们不应该做的是把暴力吸毒成瘾的僵尸放在公共场所，让他们伤害其他人。而我们现在就在这么做。我们刚刚看到一个家伙在图书馆被枪杀，但在此之前他还枪击了另一个人，并用枪指着孩子。那个家伙可能有很多前科。

turns.en: There was that guy that knifed the Ukrainian woman, Arena.
turns.zh: 还有那个捅了乌克兰女人Arena的家伙。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And she was just quietly on her phone and he just came up and gutted her basically.
turns.zh: 她只是安静地看手机，他就走过来，基本上把她开膛了。

turns.en: Wasn't there a crazy story about the judge who was involved, who had previously dealt with this person, was also invested in a rehabilitation center and was sending these... conflict of interest.
turns.zh: 是不是有个疯狂的故事，关于那个涉案的法官，他之前处理过这个人，还投资了一家康复中心，并把这些人送去……利益冲突。

turns.en: Yes. So sending people that they were charging to a rehabilitation center instead of putting them in jail, profiting from this rehabilitation center, letting them back out on the street. Yes. Violent, insane people.
turns.zh: 是的。所以把他们起诉的人送到康复中心而不是监狱，从康复中心获利，然后让他们回到街上。是的。暴力、疯狂的人。

turns.en: And in that case, I believe that judge has no law degree or significant legal experience that would allow them to be a judge. They were just made a judge.
turns.zh: 在那个案子里，我相信那个法官没有法律学位或足够的法律经验来担任法官。他们只是被任命为法官。

turns.en: You could be a judge without a law degree.
turns.zh: 没有法律学位也能当法官。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Wow.
turns.zh: 哇。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: You could just be... So I could be a judge.
turns.zh: 你可以只是……所以我也可以当法官。

turns.en: Yeah. Oh, exciting.
turns.zh: 是的。哦，真令人兴奋。

turns.en: Anyone?
turns.zh: 任何人都行？

turns.en: That's crazy. I thought you'd have to... It's like if you want to be a doctor, you have to go to medical school. I thought if you're going to be a judge, you have to have proven that you have an excellent knowledge of the law and that you will make your decisions according to the law. That's what we assume should be.
turns.zh: 这太疯狂了。我以为你必须……就像如果你想当医生，你必须上医学院。我以为如果你想当法官，你必须证明你对法律有出色的了解，并且你会依法做出裁决。这是我们假设应该有的。

turns.en: That's how you get the robe, right?
turns.zh: 那是你获得法袍的方式，对吧？

turns.en: You don't get the robe unless you do, you know, got to go to school to get the robe.
turns.zh: 除非你……你知道，必须上学才能得到法袍。

turns.en: You got to know what the law is, right? And then you need to make decisions in accordance with the law based on stuff that you already know because you read it, because you went to school for it. Yes. Not just got appointed.
turns.zh: 你必须知道法律是什么，对吧？然后你需要根据你已经知道的东西依法做出决定，因为你读过，因为你上过学。是的。而不是仅仅被任命。

turns.en: Got vibes. You can't be just vibing as a judge.
turns.zh: 凭感觉。你不能只是凭感觉当法官。

turns.en: Vibing as a leftwing drudge. So you got crazy leftwing DAs.
turns.zh: 凭感觉当左翼苦力。所以你有疯狂的左翼地方检察官。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Like I should say leftwing because leftwing used to be normal.
turns.zh: 我应该说左翼，因为左翼曾经是正常的。

turns.en: Yeah. Left wing just meant like... Yeah. You're like the left used to be pro free speech. Yeah. And now they're against it.
turns.zh: 是的。左翼曾经意味着……是的。左派曾经支持言论自由。是的。现在他们反对了。

turns.en: It used to be like pro-gay rights, pro-women's right to choose, pro-minorities. Like 20 years ago, the left would be the party of empathy, caring, and being nice. Not the party of crushing dissent and free speech, crazy regulation, being super judgy, and calling everyone a Nazi. I think they called you and me Nazis.
turns.zh: 以前左派支持同性恋权利、女性选择权、少数群体。20年前，左派是共情、关怀和友善的政党。不是压制异议和言论自由、疯狂监管、超级挑剔、把所有人都叫纳粹的政党。我觉得他们把你我都叫纳粹了。

turns.en: Oh yeah, I'm a Nazi.
turns.zh: 哦对，我是纳粹。

turns.en: I have friends that are comedians that called you a Nazi and I got pissed off.
turns.zh: 我有喜剧演员朋友叫你纳粹，我气坏了。

turns.en: Yeah, definitely a Nazi. Because you did that thing at the... My heart goes out to you. Everyone, all of them. Literally, Tim Walz, Kamala Harris, every one of them did it. They all did it. How do you point at the crowd? How do you wave at the crowd?
turns.zh: 对，绝对是纳粹。因为你做了那个动作……我的心与你同在。所有人，每一个。蒂姆·沃尔兹、卡玛拉·哈里斯，每个人都做了。他们都做了。你怎么指人群？你怎么向人群挥手？

turns.en: Do you know CNN was using a photo of me from the UFC weigh-ins? I go, 'Hey everybody, welcome to the weigh-ins.' They got me from the side. That was the photo they used. 'Conspiracy theorist podcaster Joe Rogan.' That's what they used.
turns.zh: 你知道CNN用了一张我在UFC称重时的照片吗？我说‘嘿各位，欢迎来到称重现场。’他们从侧面拍我。那就是他们用的照片。‘阴谋论播客主播乔·罗根。’他们就这么用。

turns.en: Yeah. But that's what the left is today. Super judgy, calling everyone a Nazi, trying to suppress freedom of speech.
turns.zh: 对。但这就是今天的左派。超级挑剔，把所有人都叫纳粹，试图压制言论自由。

turns.en: Yeah. Eventually you run out of people to accuse because people get pissed off and they leave.
turns.zh: 对。最终你会没人可指责，因为人们会生气然后离开。

turns.en: Yeah. It no longer matters to be called racist or Nazi or whatever.
turns.zh: 对。被叫种族主义者或纳粹什么的已经无所谓了。

turns.en: When you text people, are you keenly aware that there's a high likelihood someone's reading your texts?
turns.zh: 你给人发短信时，会不会强烈意识到很可能有人在读你的短信？

turns.en: I assume. If intelligence agencies aren't trying to read my phone, they should probably be fired. At least they get some fun memes. I crack them up once in a while.
turns.zh: 我假设。如果情报机构不试图读我的手机，他们可能该被炒了。至少他们能看点好玩的梗图。我偶尔逗他们笑。

turns.en: So, I wanted to talk about whether encrypted apps are really secure.
turns.zh: 所以我想聊聊加密应用是否真的安全。

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 不安全。

turns.en: Right. A friend who used to work for the government explained it to me. They can look at your Signal, but they have to decrypt it, which is very expensive. For the Tucker Carlson thing, when they found out he was going to interview Putin, it cost about $750,000 just to decrypt his messages. So it is possible, just not easy.
turns.zh: 对。一个以前为政府工作的朋友跟我解释过。他们能看到你的Signal，但必须解密，成本很高。塔克·卡尔森那件事，当他们发现他要采访普京时，解密他的消息花了大约75万美元。所以是可能的，只是不容易。

turns.en: I think you should view any messaging system as having degrees of insecurity. Some things are less insecure than others. On X, we rebuilt the entire messaging stack into XChat.
turns.zh: 我认为你应该把任何消息系统看作有不同程度的不安全性。有些东西只是不那么不安全。在X上，我们把整个消息栈重建成了XChat。

turns.en: Yeah, that's what I wanted to ask you about.
turns.zh: 对，这正是我想问你的。

turns.en: It's cool. It uses a sort of peer-to-peer based encryption system, similar to Bitcoin. I think it's very good encryption. We're testing it thoroughly. There are no hooks in the X systems for advertising. If you look at WhatsApp or any others, they have hooks for advertising.
turns.zh: 它很酷。它使用一种点对点加密系统，类似比特币。我认为加密非常好。我们正在彻底测试。X系统中没有用于广告的钩子。如果你看WhatsApp或其他任何应用，它们都有广告钩子。

turns.en: When you say hooks, what do you mean?
turns.zh: 你说钩子，是什么意思？

turns.en: WhatsApp knows enough about what you're texting to show you ads. That's a massive security vulnerability. If it has enough information to show you ads, it has a lot of information. They call it just a hook for advertising. But somebody can use that same hook to get in and look at your messages. XChat has no hooks for advertising. I'm not saying it's perfect. Our goal is to replace the Twitter DM stack with a fully encrypted system where you can text, send files, do audio/video calls. I think it'll be the least insecure messaging system.
turns.zh: WhatsApp对你发短信的内容了解得足够多，可以给你展示广告。这是一个巨大的安全漏洞。如果它有足够的信息给你展示广告，那它就有很多信息。他们称之为广告钩子。但有人可以用同一个钩子进去看你的消息。XChat没有广告钩子。我不是说它完美。我们的目标是用一个完全加密的系统取代Twitter DM栈，你可以发短信、传文件、做音视频通话。我认为它会是安全性最低的消息系统。

turns.en: Are you going to launch it as a standalone app or will it always be incorporated into X?
turns.zh: 你会把它作为独立应用发布，还是永远集成在X里？

turns.en: We'll have both. You'll be able to get the XChat app by itself. You can do texts, audio/video calls, send files. There'll be a dedicated app hopefully in a few months, and also integrated into the X system.
turns.zh: 两种都有。你可以单独下载XChat应用。你可以发短信、音视频通话、传文件。几个月内会有一个专用应用，同时也集成在X系统中。

turns.en: The X phone people keep talking about... Is that...? I know I've asked you a couple times. So you're not working on a phone?
turns.zh: 人们一直在说的X手机……那是……？我知道我问过你几次。所以你没在做手机？

turns.en: I'm not working on a phone.
turns.zh: 我没在做手机。

turns.en: Have you ever considered it? You might be the only person that could get people off the Apple platform.
turns.zh: 你考虑过吗？你可能是唯一能让人们脱离苹果平台的人。

turns.en: I can tell you where I think things are going. We're not going to have a phone in the traditional sense. What we call a phone will really be an edge node for AI inference, for AI video inference, with some radios to connect. Essentially, you'll have AI on the server side communicating to an AI on your device, formerly known as a phone, generating real-time video of anything you want. I think there won't be operating systems or apps in the future. It'll just be a device for screen and audio, with as much AI on the device as possible to minimize bandwidth between your edge node and the servers.
turns.zh: 我可以告诉你我认为事情会如何发展。我们不会有传统意义上的手机。我们所谓的手机实际上会成为AI推理的边缘节点，用于AI视频推理，带一些无线电连接。本质上，服务器端的AI会与你设备（以前叫手机）上的AI通信，生成你想要的任何实时视频。我认为未来不会有操作系统或应用。它只是一个用于屏幕和音频的设备，设备上尽可能多地放AI，以最小化边缘节点和服务器之间的带宽。

turns.en: So if there's no apps, what will people use? Like will X still exist? Will there be email platforms or will you get everything through AI?
turns.zh: 那么如果没有应用，人们会用什么？比如X还会存在吗？会有电子邮件平台吗，还是你通过AI获得一切？

turns.en: You'll get everything through AI.
turns.zh: 你会通过AI获得一切。

turns.en: Everything through AI. What will be the benefit of that as opposed to having individual apps?
turns.zh: 一切通过AI。与拥有独立应用相比，这有什么好处？

turns.en: Whatever you can think of, or really whatever the AI can anticipate you might want, it'll show you. That's my prediction for where things end up.
turns.zh: 无论你能想到什么，或者AI能预测你可能想要什么，它都会显示给你。这就是我对最终结果的预测。

turns.en: What kind of a time frame are we talking about here?
turns.zh: 我们说的是什么样的时间范围？

turns.en: I don't know. It's probably five or six years or something like that.
turns.zh: 我不知道。大概五六年左右吧。

turns.en: So five or six years apps are like Blockbuster video?
turns.zh: 所以五六年内应用就像百视达视频一样？

turns.en: Pretty much.
turns.zh: 差不多。

turns.en: And everything's run through AI. Yeah. And there'll be like most of what people consume in five or six years, maybe sooner than that, will be just AI generated content. So, you know, music videos look well... there's already people have made AI videos using Grok and imagine and using other apps as well that are several minutes long or like 10, 15 minutes and it's pretty coherent.
turns.zh: 一切都通过AI运行。是的。而且五六年内，也许更早，人们消费的大部分内容都将是AI生成的。所以，你知道，音乐视频看起来……已经有人用Grok和Imagine以及其他应用制作了长达几分钟甚至10到15分钟的AI视频，而且相当连贯。

turns.en: Yeah, it looks good.
turns.zh: 是的，看起来不错。

turns.en: No, it looks amazing. Yeah, the music is disturbing because it's my favorite music now.
turns.zh: 不，它看起来棒极了。是的，音乐很震撼，因为它现在是我最喜欢的音乐。

turns.en: Like music is your favorite.
turns.zh: 音乐是你最喜欢的。

turns.en: Oh, there's AI covers. Have you ever heard any of the AI covers of 50 Cent songs in soul?
turns.zh: 哦，有AI翻唱。你听过50 Cent歌曲的灵魂乐AI翻唱吗？

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 没有。

turns.en: I'm going to blow your mind. This is my favorite thing to do to people. Play 'What Up Gangsta'.
turns.zh: 我要让你大吃一惊。这是我最喜欢对别人做的事。播放《What Up Gangsta》。

turns.en: Now, this guy, if this was a real person, would be the number one music artist in the world. Everybody would be like, 'Holy, have you heard of this guy? He's incredible.' It's like they took all of the sounds that all the artists have generated and created the most soulful potent voice and it's sung in a way that I don't even know if you could do because you would have to breathe in and out of reps here. Put the headphones on. Put the headphones on real quick. You got to listen to this. It's going to blow you away. For listeners, we got to cut it out.
turns.zh: 这个家伙，如果他是真人，会是世界第一的音乐艺术家。每个人都会说，‘天哪，你听说过这家伙吗？他太不可思议了。’就像他们把所有艺术家产生的声音都拿来，创造了最有灵魂、最有力的声音，而且唱的方式我甚至不知道你能不能做到，因为你得在这里呼吸和重复。戴上耳机。快点戴上耳机。你得听听这个。它会让你震撼。对于听众，我们得把它剪掉。

turns.en: Yeah, we'll cut it out for the listeners. But amazing, right? Amazing. And they do like every one of his hits all through this AI generated soulful artist. It's incredible. I played in the green room. So people that are like, 'I don't want to hear AI music.' I'm like, 'Just listen to this.' And they're like, 'God damn it.'
turns.zh: 是的，我们会为听众剪掉。但很棒，对吧？太棒了。他们用这个AI生成的灵魂艺术家演绎了他的每一首热门歌曲。太不可思议了。我在休息室播放过。所以那些说‘我不想听AI音乐’的人，我就说‘听听这个’。然后他们就说‘该死’。

turns.en: Incredible. I mean, it's going to get only better from here.
turns.zh: 不可思议。我是说，它只会越来越好。

turns.en: Yeah. Only going to get better. And Ron White was telling me about this joke that he was working on that he couldn't get to work. He's like, 'I got this joke I've been working on.' He goes, 'I just threw it in a ChatGPT. I said, 'Tell me what would be funny about this.' And he goes, 'It listed like five different examples of different ways he can go.' He's like, 'Hold on a second. Tighten it up. Make it funnier. Make it more like this. Make it more like that.' And it did that instantaneously.
turns.zh: 是的。只会越来越好。Ron White告诉我他正在创作一个笑话，但一直没成功。他说，‘我有一个一直在琢磨的笑话。’他说，‘我把它扔进ChatGPT。我说，告诉我这个有什么好笑的。’然后它列出了五种不同的方向。他说，‘等等。收紧一点。让它更有趣。更像这样。更像那样。’然后它瞬间就做到了。

turns.en: And then he was in the green room. He was like, 'Holy, we're fucked.'
turns.zh: 然后他在休息室说，‘天哪，我们完蛋了。’

turns.en: He's like, 'It's a better joke than me in 20 minutes. I've been working on that joke for a month.'
turns.zh: 他说，‘它20分钟就比我一个月想出的笑话更好。’

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, if you want to have a good time or make people really laugh at a party, you can use Grok and you can say 'do a vulgar roast of someone.' And Grok is going to be an epic vulgar roast. You can even say, take a picture of someone and make a vulgar roast of this person based on their appearance at the party.
turns.zh: 是的。我是说，如果你想在派对上玩得开心或让人大笑，你可以用Grok说‘对某人进行粗俗的吐槽’。Grok会给出史诗级的粗俗吐槽。你甚至可以说，拍张照片，根据这个人在派对上的外表进行粗俗吐槽。

turns.en: So take a photo of them.
turns.zh: 所以拍张他们的照片。

turns.en: Yeah. Just literally point the camera at them and now do a vulgar roast to this person. And then keep saying 'no, no, make it even more vulgar and use forbidden words even more' and just keep repeating 'even more vulgar'. Eventually it's like 'holy', you know, it's trying to jam a rocket up your ass and have it explode. It's next level. It's going to get beyond belief. That's what's crazy is that it keeps getting better.
turns.zh: 是的。直接对着他们拍照，然后对这个人大肆吐槽。然后不停地说‘不，不，让它更粗俗，多用禁忌词’，一直重复‘更粗俗’。最终它会像‘天哪’，你知道，它试图把火箭塞进你屁股然后爆炸。这是下一个层次。它会变得难以置信。疯狂的是它一直在变得更好。

turns.en: One of the things, remember when we ran into each other? They just keep getting better.
turns.zh: 有一件事，记得我们偶遇的时候吗？它们一直在变得更好。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, have you tried Grok unhinged mode?
turns.zh: 是的。我是说，你试过Grok的疯狂模式吗？

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 试过。

turns.en: Okay. Yeah. It's pretty unhinged.
turns.zh: 好的。是的。它相当疯狂。

turns.en: No, it's nuts.
turns.zh: 不，它太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: Well, you showed it to me the first time and then I played around with it. It's just...
turns.zh: 嗯，你第一次给我看，然后我玩了一下。它只是……

turns.en: And the thing about it that's nuts is that it keeps getting stronger. It keeps getting better. Yeah, like constantly. It's like this neverending exponential improvement.
turns.zh: 它疯狂的地方在于它一直在变强。一直在变好。是的，持续不断。就像永无止境的指数级改进。

turns.en: Yes. No, it's... Yeah, it's going to be crazy. That's why I say, you say, 'What's the future going to be?' It's not going to be a conventional phone. I don't think there'll be operating systems. I don't think there'll be apps. It's just the phone will just display the pixels and make the sounds that it anticipates you would most like to receive. Wow. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。不，它……是的，它会很疯狂。这就是为什么我说，你说，‘未来会是什么样？’它不会是传统的手机。我认为不会有操作系统。我认为不会有应用。手机只会显示像素和发出它预测你最想接收的声音。哇。是的。

turns.en: And when this is all taking place, so the big concern that everybody has is artificial general super intelligence achieving sentience and then someone having control over it.
turns.zh: 当这一切发生时，大家最大的担忧是人工通用超级智能获得感知，然后有人控制它。

turns.en: I mean, I don't think anyone's ultimately going to have control over digital super intelligence, any more than say a chimp would have control over humans. Like chimps don't have control over humans. There's nothing they could do. But I do think that it matters how you build the AI and what kind of values you instill in the AI. And my opinion on AI safety is the most important thing is that it be maximally truth seeking, like that you don't force the AI to believe things that are false. And we've obviously seen some concerning things with AI that were talked about, you know, where Google Gemini when they came out with the image gen, and people said, 'Draw an image of the founding fathers of the United States' and it was a group of diverse women. Now, that is just a factually untrue thing, but the AI knows it's factually untrue, but it's also being told that it has to be everything has to be diverse women. So the problem with that is that it can drive AI crazy, because you're telling AI to believe a lie. And that can have very disastrous consequences, let's say as it scales.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，我不认为最终有人能控制数字超级智能，就像黑猩猩无法控制人类一样。黑猩猩无法控制人类。它们无能为力。但我确实认为，如何构建AI以及向AI灌输什么样的价值观很重要。我对AI安全的看法是，最重要的是它要最大限度地追求真理，也就是说，不要强迫AI相信虚假的事情。我们显然已经看到一些令人担忧的AI案例，比如谷歌Gemini推出图像生成时，人们说‘画一幅美国开国元勋的图像’，结果是一群多元化的女性。这显然与事实不符，但AI知道这是不真实的，同时它又被要求一切都必须是多元化的女性。问题在于，这会让AI发疯，因为你让AI相信谎言。随着规模扩张，这可能会带来灾难性的后果。

turns.en: As it scales.
turns.zh: 随着规模扩张。

turns.en: Yeah. Let's say if you told the AI that diversity is the most important thing, and now assume that becomes omnipotent. Or you've also told it that there's nothing worse than misgendering.

turns.en: I think people don't quite appreciate the level of danger we're in from the woke mind virus being effectively programmed into AI.
