turns.zh: 是的，这是一个噩梦般的狂热梦境，人们终于从中醒来。现在，当你开始接触 Doge 的事情，开始发现有多少钱被转移给非政府组织，涉及多少钱，以及完全无法追踪的资金，这又是两年前你不知道的事情。

turns.en: No, I was aware of it. I just didn't realize how big it was. Just how much waste and fraud there is in the government is truly vast. In fact, the government didn't even know, nor did they care.
turns.zh: 不，我知道。我只是没意识到它有多大。政府中的浪费和欺诈真是巨大。事实上，政府甚至不知道，也不在乎。

turns.en: That's crazy.
turns.zh: 那太疯狂了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And I mean just like some of the very basic stuff that Doge did will have lasting effects. And some of these things like they're so elementary you can't believe it. So, the Doge team got the main payments computers to require the congressional appropriation code. So, when a payment is made, you have to actually enter the congressional appropriation code. That used to be optional and often would be just left blank. So, the money would just go out, but it wasn't even tied to a congressional appropriation. Then they also made the comment field for the payment mandatory. So you have to say something. We're not saying that what is said like you can say anything. You your cat could run across the keyboard. You could go 'qwerty ASDF' but you have to say something above nothing because what we found was that there were tens of billions maybe hundreds of billions of dollars that were zombie payments. So like somebody had approved a payment in the government and it was a recurring payment and they retired or died or changed jobs and no one turned the money off.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，就像 Doge 做的一些非常基本的事情会产生持久的影响。其中一些事情如此基础，你简直不敢相信。所以，Doge 团队让主要的支付计算机要求输入国会拨款代码。所以，当进行支付时，你必须实际输入国会拨款代码。以前这是可选的，而且经常留空。所以钱就出去了，但甚至没有与国会拨款挂钩。然后他们还让支付的备注字段成为必填项。所以你必须说点什么。我们不是说说了什么，你可以说任何东西。你的猫可以在键盘上跑过。你可以输入‘qwerty ASDF’，但你必须说点什么，而不是什么都不说，因为我们发现有数百亿甚至数千亿美元是僵尸支付。就像有人在政府中批准了一笔支付，那是一笔定期支付，然后他们退休、去世或换了工作，没有人关掉这笔钱。

turns.en: So the money would just keep going out, and it's a pretty rare company or individual who will complain that they're getting money that they should not get. And a bunch of the money was just going to the states as transfer payments. So these are automatic payments with no accounting for them at all. I imagine like there's an automatic debit of your credit card and you never look at the statement. So it's just money going out. That's why I call them zombie payments. They might have been legitimate at one point, but the person who approved that recurring payment changed jobs, died, retired, or whatever, and no one ever turned the money off. My guess is that's probably at least a hundred billion a year, maybe 200. And going where? There are millions of these payments. Millions of payments going to who knows where. In a bunch of cases, there are fraud rings that operate professionally to exploit the system. They figure out some security hole and just do professional fraud. That's where we found, for example, people who were 300 years old in the Social Security Administration database. I thought this was a mistake of not registering their deaths, that people were born a long time ago and it defaulted to a certain number, so after time those people were still in the system. It was just an error in the accounting. But that's not true. One of two things must be true: either there's a typo or mistake in the computer, or it's fraudulent. We don't have any 300-year-old vampires living in America. Allegedly. And we don't have people receiving payments who were born in the future. Really? Yes. There are people receiving payments whose birth date was like in 2100 something. Like next century. So we know one of two things must be true: either there's a mistake in the computer or it's fraud. If you have someone's birth date that's either in the future or where they are older than the oldest living American (the oldest living American is 114 years old), then if they're more than 114, there is either a mistake and someone should call them and say, 'I think we have your birthday wrong because it says you were born in 1786, and that was before there was really an America, we were still fighting England.' This person either needs to be in the Guinness Book of World Records or they're not alive. But still, at the end of the day, money is going towards that account connected to this person that is either non-existent or dead. So there were something like 20 million people in the Social Security Administration database that could not possibly be alive based on their birth date. To be clear, 20 million people that were receiving funds? A bunch of them, most were not receiving funds. Some were. But let me tell you how the scam works. It's a bank shot. The Social Security Administration database is used as the source of truth by all other government databases. So even if they stop payments on the Social Security Administration database, unemployment insurance, Small Business Administration, student loans all check that database to see if this is a legitimate alive person. And if the Social Security database says yes, this person is still alive even though they're 200 years old, but forgets to mention their age, it just returns 'yes, this person is alive.' Then they can exploit the entire rest of the government ecosystem. So you get fake student loans, fake unemployment insurance, fake medical payments. This doesn't have to be tied to an individual with an address you can check. No, if you did any check at all, you would stop this. So there are multiple layers. The Social Security number verifies this is a real person, and then other systems check every other government payment system for everything: Small Business Administration, student loans, Medicaid, Medicare, every other government payment system—there are hundreds—can all be exploited as long as the Social Security database says this person is alive. That's the nature of the scam. It's a bank shot. The rebuttal from the Dems is like, 'Oh, the vast majority of people marked as alive in Social Security weren't receiving Social Security payments.' That is true. What they forgot to mention is they're getting fraudulent payments from every other government program. That's why the Dems were so opposed to turning off—to declaring someone dead who was dead—because it would stop all the other fraud from happening. But all this is trackable. If they wanted to, they could chase it all down. It's not even hard. And yet they're opposing chasing it all down because it turns off the money magnet for the illegals. It's very logical. I'm saying the most common sense things possible. If someone has a birthday in Social Security that is impossible—older than the oldest living American or born in the future—then you should call them and say, 'Excuse me, we seem to have your birthday wrong, because it says you're 200 years old.'
turns.zh: 所以钱会一直往外流，很少有公司或个人会抱怨自己拿到了不该拿的钱。而且很多钱只是作为转移支付流向了各州。这些是自动付款，完全没有会计记录。想象一下，就像你的信用卡自动扣款，你从来不看账单。钱就这么流出去了。所以我管它们叫僵尸付款。它们可能曾经是合法的，但批准这笔定期付款的人换了工作、去世、退休或别的什么，没人关掉这笔钱。我猜这至少每年一千亿，可能两千亿。钱流向了哪里？有数百万笔这样的付款。数百万笔付款流向了天知道的地方。在很多情况下，有诈骗团伙专业运作，利用系统漏洞。他们找到某个安全漏洞，然后进行专业诈骗。比如我们就在社会保障局的数据库里发现了300岁的人。我原以为这是没有登记死亡的错误，人们出生很久了，系统默认了一个数字，所以这些人还在系统里。这只是会计方式的一个错误。但事实并非如此。只有两种可能：要么是电脑里有笔误或错误，要么就是欺诈。美国没有300岁的吸血鬼。据称。而且也没有人收到未来出生的人的付款。真的吗？是的。有些收款人的出生日期是2100年以后的。比如下个世纪。所以我们知道只有两种可能：要么是电脑错误，要么是欺诈。如果一个人的出生日期在未来，或者比美国最长寿的人（114岁）还老，那么如果超过114岁，要么是错误，应该有人打电话说：‘我觉得你的生日搞错了，因为上面写你是1786年出生的，那时候美国还没真正成立，我们还在和英国打仗。’这个人要么该进吉尼斯世界纪录，要么已经死了。但最终，钱还是流向了这个人的账户，而这个人要么不存在，要么已经死了。所以社会保障局的数据库里大约有2000万人，根据出生日期不可能还活着。明确一下，2000万人都在收款？大部分没有收款，有些在收。但让我告诉你这个骗局是怎么运作的。这是一个间接攻击。社会保障局的数据库被所有其他政府数据库用作真相来源。所以即使他们停止了社会保障局的付款，失业保险、小企业管理局、学生贷款都会检查那个数据库，看这个人是否合法活着。如果社会保障数据库说这个人还活着，即使他200岁，但忘了提年龄，只返回‘是，这个人活着’，那么他们就可以利用整个政府生态系统。于是就有了假学生贷款、假失业保险、假医疗付款。这不需要关联到有地址可查的个人。不，只要做任何检查，就能阻止。所以有多层。社会保障号验证这是一个真人，然后其他系统检查所有其他政府付款系统：小企业管理局、学生贷款、医疗补助、医疗保险，以及其他数百个政府付款系统，只要社会保障数据库说这个人活着，就都能被利用。这就是骗局的本质，一个间接攻击。民主党的反驳是：‘哦，社会保障局标记为活着的人中，绝大多数并没有领取社会保障局的付款。’这没错。但他们忘了提，这些人从其他每个政府项目领取欺诈性付款。这就是为什么民主党如此反对宣布已死的人死亡，因为这会阻止所有其他欺诈。但所有这些都可以追踪。如果他们想，完全可以追查到底。这甚至不难。但他们却反对追查，因为这会切断非法移民的财源。这很符合逻辑。我说的都是最常识性的事情。如果一个人的生日在社会保障局里是不可能的——比美国最长寿的人还老，或者在未来——那么你应该打电话说：‘不好意思，我们好像搞错了你的生日，因为上面说你200岁了。’

turns.en: That's all you need to do. And then you would remove them from the social security database and make that number no longer available for all those other government payments.
turns.zh: 你只需要这么做。然后把他们从社保数据库中移除，让那个号码不再用于其他政府支付。

turns.en: Exactly.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Wow. And how much money are we talking?
turns.zh: 哇。那涉及多少钱？

turns.en: It's hundreds of billions of dollars.
turns.zh: 数千亿美元。

turns.en: And this is all traceable. Like you could hunt all—like you don't need to be Sherlock Holmes here is what I'm saying.
turns.zh: 而且这都是可追溯的。就像你可以追查所有——我是说，你不需要是福尔摩斯。

turns.en: Well, this we don't need to call Sherlock Holmes for this one. Is this part of—you just need to call the person and say, 'Excuse me, we seem to have your birthday wrong because it says you're 200 years old or were born in the future. So could you tell us what your birthday is?' That's all you need to do. It's that simple.
turns.zh: 嗯，这个我们不需要请福尔摩斯。你只需要给那个人打电话说：‘不好意思，我们好像把你的生日搞错了，因为系统显示你200岁了，或者出生在未来。你能告诉我们你的生日吗？’你只需要这么做。就这么简单。

turns.en: But all these other government payments that are available that are connected to this social security number, it seems like if you just chased that all down, you would find the widespread fraud. You would find where it's going.
turns.zh: 但所有其他与这个社保号相关的政府支付，如果你追查下去，似乎就会发现广泛的欺诈。你会找到钱的去向。

turns.en: Yes. The root of the problem is the Social Security Administration database because the social security number in the United States is used as a de facto national ID number. That's why the bank always asks for your social, any financial institution will ask for your social security number.
turns.zh: 是的。问题的根源是社会保障管理局的数据库，因为在美国，社保号被用作事实上的国民身份证号码。这就是为什么银行总是要你的社保号，任何金融机构都会要你的社保号。

turns.en: This sounds so insane that this isn't chased down. I mean that in and of itself is such mishandling.
turns.zh: 这听起来太疯狂了，居然没人追查。我的意思是，这本身就是严重的处理不当。

turns.en: Yes. No, it's mind-blowing. So yeah, it's crazy.
turns.zh: 是的。不，这令人震惊。所以，确实很疯狂。

turns.en: Well, you were very reluctant last time you were here to talk about the extent of some of the fraud because you're like, 'They could kill me because this is kind of—'
turns.zh: 嗯，你上次来的时候很不愿意谈论某些欺诈的程度，因为你好像说：‘他们可能会杀了我，因为这有点——’

turns.en: Oh, what? Yeah. What I was saying is that if you create—I like to be pragmatic and realistic. You actually can't manage to zero fraud. You can manage to low fraud number but not to zero fraud. If you manage to zero fraud, you're going to push so many people over the edge who are receiving fraudulent payments that the number of inbound homicidal maniacs will be really hard to overcome. So I'm taking a quite reasonable position, which is that we should simply reduce the amount of fraud, which I think is not an extremist position. And we should aspire to have less fraud over time. Not that we should be ultra draconian and eliminate every last scrap of fraud, which I guess would be nice to have, but we don't even need to go that extreme. I'm saying we should just stop the blatant large scale super obvious fraud.
turns.zh: 哦，什么？是的。我是说，如果你创造——我喜欢务实和现实。你实际上无法实现零欺诈。你可以把欺诈降到很低，但无法降到零。如果你实现零欺诈，你会把很多接受欺诈性付款的人逼到绝境，导致涌入的杀人狂数量难以应对。所以我采取了一个相当合理的立场，那就是我们应该减少欺诈的数量，我认为这不是极端立场。我们应该随着时间的推移努力减少欺诈。而不是极端严厉地消除每一丝欺诈，那当然很好，但我们甚至不需要那么极端。我是说，我们应该停止那些明目张胆的大规模超级明显的欺诈。

turns.en: I think that's a reasonable position.
turns.zh: 我认为这是一个合理的立场。

turns.en: It's a very reasonable position. Yeah. And so what was the most shocking pushback that you got when you started implementing Doge? When you started investigating into where money was going?
turns.zh: 这是一个非常合理的立场。是的。那么，当你开始实施Doge时，当你开始调查资金去向时，你遇到的最令人震惊的阻力是什么？

turns.en: Well, I guess this was—I should have anticipated this, but while most of the fraudulent government payments, especially to the NGOs, go to the Democrats—most of it, let's say 80% maybe 90%—10 to 20% of it does go to Republicans. And so when we'd turn off funding to a fraudulent NGO, we'd get complaints from whatever the 10% of Republicans who were receiving the money, and they would very loudly complain. Because the honest answer is the Republicans are partly receiving some of the fraud too. They're getting a big—
turns.zh: 嗯，我想这是——我本应预料到的，但虽然大多数欺诈性政府支付，尤其是给非政府组织的，流向了民主党——大部分，比如说80%可能90%——其中10%到20%确实流向了共和党。所以当我们切断对一个欺诈性非政府组织的资助时，我们会收到那10%接受资金的共和党人的投诉，他们会非常大声地抱怨。因为诚实的答案是，共和党人也部分地接受了欺诈资金。他们得到了很大一部分——

turns.en: Jesus. Yeah, I want to be clear. It's not like the Republican party is some ultra pure paragon of virtue here.
turns.zh: 天哪。是的，我想说清楚。共和党并不是什么超级纯洁的美德典范。

turns.en: No.
turns.zh: 不。

turns.en: Okay.
turns.zh: 好的。

turns.en: Well, you see that with the congressional insider trading. It's across the board.
turns.zh: 嗯，你在国会内幕交易中就能看到。这是普遍现象。

turns.en: It's left and right.
turns.zh: 左派和右派都有。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, the whole uni-party criticism has some validity to it. So if you turn off fraudulent payments, it's not like 100% of those payments were going to Democrats. A small percentage were also going to Republicans. Those Republicans complained very loudly. And so there was a lot of pushback on the Republican side when we started cutting some of these funds. I tried telling them, 'Well, 90% of the money is going to your opponents,' but they still—even if they're getting 10%—
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，对整个两党一致的批评有一定道理。所以如果你切断欺诈性支付，并不是100%都流向了民主党。一小部分也流向了共和党。那些共和党人非常大声地抱怨。所以当我们开始削减一些资金时，共和党方面有很多阻力。我试图告诉他们：‘嗯，90%的钱都流向了你们的对手，’但他们仍然——即使他们只得到10%——

turns.en: They want their piece.
turns.zh: 他们想要自己那份。

turns.en: Yeah. They want their piece, and they've been getting that piece for a long time.
turns.zh: 是的。他们想要自己那份，而且他们已经拿了很久了。

turns.en: Did you see—this is why politics is dirty business.
turns.zh: 你看到了吗——这就是为什么政治是肮脏的勾当。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, that's like saying if you like sausages and respect the law, do not watch either of them being made.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，这就像说如果你喜欢香肠并尊重法律，就不要看它们是怎么做的。

turns.en: Yeah. Wow. Well, that's not even true because I've made sausage before. It's actually not that big a deal. Fat and spices and casing, run it through the machine. Not that big a deal.
turns.zh: 是的。哇。嗯，这甚至不准确，因为我以前做过香肠。其实没那么大不了。肥肉、香料和肠衣，放进机器里。没那么大不了。

turns.en: Yeah. But I think the stuff I'm saying here is not—if you stand back and think about it for a second, like, 'Oh yeah, that makes sense.' It's not like one political party is going to be pure devil or pure angel. I think there's much more corruption on the Democrat side, but there's still some corruption on the Republican side.
turns.zh: 是的。但我认为我在这里说的东西并不是——如果你退后一步想一想，就像‘哦，是的，那有道理。’并不是一个政党会是纯粹的魔鬼或纯粹的天使。我认为民主党那边的腐败要多得多，但共和党那边仍然有一些腐败。

turns.en: How did it happen that the majority of the corruption wound up being on the Democrat side?
turns.zh: 为什么大多数腐败最终都发生在民主党这边？

turns.en: Well, because the transfer payments, especially to illegals, are very much on the Democrat side.
turns.zh: 嗯，因为转移支付，尤其是给非法移民的，很大程度上在民主党这边。

turns.en: So that's the root of it all is the illegal situation.
turns.zh: 所以这一切的根源就是非法移民问题。

turns.en: Yes. I mean, there's—
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，还有——

turns.en: Or a focal point.
turns.zh: 或者说一个焦点。

turns.en: Yes. It's also accurate to say that while obviously not everyone who is a Democrat is a criminal, almost everyone who is a criminal is a Democrat because the Democrats are the soft crime party. So if you're a criminal, who are you going to vote for? The soft crime party.
turns.zh: 是的。也可以准确地说，虽然显然不是每个民主党人都是罪犯，但几乎每个罪犯都是民主党人，因为民主党是纵容犯罪的政党。所以如果你是个罪犯，你会投票给谁？纵容犯罪的政党。

turns.en: Right. Right.
turns.zh: 对。对。

turns.en: Did you think you were going to be able to get more done than you were?
turns.zh: 你原本以为自己能完成更多吗？

turns.en: We did get a lot done, right? And Doge is still happening, by the way. The Doge is still underway. There are still waste and fraud being cut by the Doge team. So it hasn't stopped.
turns.zh: 我们确实完成了很多，对吧？而且顺便说一句，Doge仍在进行中。Doge仍在进行中。Doge团队仍在削减浪费和欺诈。所以它没有停止。

turns.en: It's less publicized.
turns.zh: 它不那么受关注了。

turns.en: It's less publicized. And they don't have a clear person to attack anymore.
turns.zh: 它不那么受关注了。而且他们再也没有一个明确的攻击目标了。

turns.en: Well, it seems like they basically applied immense pressure to me to just stop it. So then I'm like the best thing for me is to just cut out of this. In any case, as a special government employee, I could only be there for like 120 days anyway, something like that. So whatever the law says. So I necessarily could only be there for 4 months as a special government employee. So, but yeah, I mean you turn off the money spigot to fraudsters, they get very upset to say the least. And my death threat level went ballistic, you know, like a rocket going to orbit. So now that I'm not in DC, I guess they don't really have a person to attack anymore.
turns.zh: 嗯，基本上他们对我施加了巨大压力，让我停止这件事。所以我觉得最好的办法就是退出。无论如何，作为特别政府雇员，我也只能待大概120天，法律就是这么规定的。所以我作为特别政府雇员最多只能待4个月。但是，当你切断欺诈者的资金来源时，他们至少会非常不满。我的死亡威胁级别飙升，就像火箭升空一样。所以现在我不在华盛顿了，我想他们也没有攻击目标了。

turns.en: Well, the rhetoric about you has calmed down significantly.
turns.zh: 嗯，关于你的言论已经明显缓和了。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: It was disturbing. It was disturbing to watch. It was like this is crazy. And to watch these politicians engage in it and all these people just like framing you as this monster. I was like this is so weird. Like this is what happens when you uncover fraud. The whole machine turns on you. And if it wasn't for a person like you who owns a platform and has an enormous amount of money, like could have destroyed you.
turns.zh: 这很令人不安。看着这一切很不安。就像疯了。看到那些政客参与其中，所有人都把你描绘成怪物。我觉得这太奇怪了。这就是揭露欺诈的下场。整个机器都针对你。如果不是像你这样拥有平台和巨额财富的人，你早就被毁了。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: And that was the goal.
turns.zh: 那就是目标。

turns.en: The goal was to destroy me. Absolutely.
turns.zh: 目标就是摧毁我。绝对是的。

turns.en: Because you were getting in the way of this amazing graft.
turns.zh: 因为你挡了这巨大贪污的路。

turns.en: The gigantic fraud machine.
turns.zh: 这个巨大的欺诈机器。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: I think the DOGE team has done a lot of good work. In terms of fraud and waste prevented, my guess is it's probably on the order of 200 or 300 billion a year. So it's pretty good.
turns.zh: 我认为DOGE团队做了很多出色的工作。在防止欺诈和浪费方面，我猜每年大概有两三千亿美元。所以相当不错。

turns.en: What do you think could have been done if you just had full reign and total cooperation? How much do you think you could have saved?
turns.zh: 如果你有完全的控制权和全面合作，你认为能做什么？你觉得能节省多少？

turns.en: I mean, what level of power are we assuming here?
turns.zh: 我们假设什么级别的权力？

turns.en: Godlike.
turns.zh: 神一般的权力。

turns.en: Oh, yeah. I probably cut the federal budget in half and get more done. It's so crazy that you can get more done with half the federal budget. It's that widespread.
turns.zh: 哦，是的。我大概能把联邦预算削减一半，还能做得更多。用一半的联邦预算做得更多，这太疯狂了。浪费就是这么普遍。

turns.en: Well, I mean a whole bunch of government departments simply shouldn't exist in my opinion. Like examples?
turns.zh: 嗯，我认为很多政府部门根本不应该存在。比如？

turns.en: Well, the Department of Education, which was created recently under Jimmy Carter. Our educational results have gone downhill ever since it was created. So if you create a department and the result is a massive decline in educational results, and it's the Department of Education, you're better off not having it because we did better before there was one than after.
turns.zh: 嗯，教育部，是在吉米·卡特时期最近成立的。自成立以来，我们的教育成果一直在下滑。所以如果你设立一个部门，结果却是教育成果大幅下降，而且这还是教育部，那还不如没有，因为我们在它成立之前做得更好。

turns.en: When you let the states run it.
turns.zh: 当你让各州来管理的时候。

turns.en: Yes. Because at least the states can compete with one another. But the problem is like cutting Department of Education. Our kids need education. Yeah, they do. But this is a new department that didn't even exist until the late 70s. And ever since that department was created, the educational results have declined. So why would you have an institution continue that has made education worse? It doesn't make sense.
turns.zh: 是的。因为至少各州可以相互竞争。但问题在于削减教育部。我们的孩子需要教育。是的，他们需要。但这是一个直到70年代末才存在的新部门。自从这个部门成立以来，教育成果一直在下降。所以为什么要让一个使教育变得更糟的机构继续存在？这说不通。

turns.en: They killed it though, right? No, there still unfortunately. But they were trying to kill it.
turns.zh: 但他们把它取消了吗？不，不幸的是还在。但他们曾试图取消它。

turns.en: It has been substantially reduced.
turns.zh: 它已经被大幅削减了。

turns.en: Okay. What other organizations? What other departments?
turns.zh: 好的。还有其他什么组织？什么部门？

turns.en: Well, I'm a small government guy. So when the country was created, we just had the Department of State, Department of War, the Department of Justice, we had an Attorney General, and Treasury Department. I don't know why you need more than that.
turns.zh: 嗯，我是小政府支持者。所以当国家成立时，我们只有国务院、战争部、司法部、总检察长和财政部。我不知道为什么需要更多。

turns.en: So what other departments specifically do you think are just completely ineffective?
turns.zh: 那么具体来说，你认为哪些部门完全无效？

turns.en: Well, it's a philosophical question of how much government you think there should be. In my opinion, there should be the least amount of government. I've heard the most bizarre argument against this is that you're cutting jobs and you're gonna leave people jobless. And I'm like, but their jobs are useless. Paying people to do nothing doesn't make sense. There's a great story about Milton Friedman. Someone complained to him that people were digging ditches with shovels, and Friedman said, 'Well, I think you should use excavating equipment instead of shovels and you could get it done with far fewer people.' And then someone said, 'But then we're going to lose a lot of jobs.' Friedman said, 'Well, in that case, why don't you have them use teaspoons? Just dig ditches with teaspoons. Think of all the jobs you'll create.'
turns.zh: 嗯，这是一个哲学问题，你认为应该有多少政府。在我看来，政府应该尽可能小。我听到最奇怪的反对意见是，你削减工作会让人们失业。但我想，他们的工作是无用的。付钱让人什么都不做没有意义。有一个关于米尔顿·弗里德曼的精彩故事。有人向他抱怨说人们用铁锹挖沟，弗里德曼说：‘嗯，我认为你应该用挖掘设备而不是铁锹，这样可以用更少的人完成。’然后有人说：‘但那样我们会失去很多工作。’弗里德曼说：‘那么，为什么不让他们用茶匙呢？就用茶匙挖沟。想想你会创造多少工作。’

turns.en: I mean, basically you just want people to work on things that are productive. You want people to work on building things, providing products and services that people find valuable, like making food, being a farmer or a plumber or electrician, or anyone who's a builder or providing useful services. That's what you want people to be doing, not fake government jobs that don't add any value or may subtract value. There's also the absurdity of how the economy is measured. The way economists measure the economy is nonsensical because they'll measure any job no matter if that job is dumb and has no point and is even counterproductive. So the joke is like: two economists go on a hike in the woods and come across a pile of shit. One economist says to the other, 'I'll pay you $100 to eat that shit.' The economist eats it, gets the $100. They keep walking. Then they come across another pile of shit. The other economist says, 'Now I'll pay you $100 to eat the pile of shit.' So he pays the other economist $100, he eats the pile of shit. Then they realize: wait a second, we both just ate a pile of shit, and we're no better off, we don't have any extra money, you just gave the $100 back to me, and we both ate a pile of shit. This doesn't make any sense.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，基本上你只是希望人们从事生产性的工作。你希望人们从事建设性工作，提供人们认为有价值的产品和服务，比如制作食物，成为农民、水管工或电工，或者任何建设者或提供有用服务的人。这就是你希望人们做的事情，而不是那些不增加价值甚至可能减少价值的虚假政府工作。还有衡量经济的方式的荒谬性。经济学家衡量经济的方式毫无意义，因为他们会衡量任何工作，无论那份工作多么愚蠢、毫无意义甚至适得其反。所以有个笑话：两个经济学家在树林里徒步，遇到一堆屎。一个经济学家对另一个说：‘我给你100美元，你吃掉那堆屎。’那个经济学家吃了，拿到100美元。他们继续走。然后他们又遇到一堆屎。另一个经济学家说：‘现在我给你100美元，你吃掉那堆屎。’于是他付给另一个经济学家100美元，他吃了那堆屎。然后他们意识到：等等，我们都吃了一堆屎，而且我们没有任何改善，我们没有多余的钱，你只是把100美元还给了我，我们都吃了一堆屎。这毫无意义。

transcript.sec: Debt crisis and the role of AI

turns.en: And they said, "No, no, but think of the economy because that's $200 of that in the economy that that basically measure eating eating would count as a job." This is to illustrate the absurdity of economics.
turns.zh: 他们说：‘不，不，但想想经济，因为那200美元在经济中，基本上衡量吃饭都会算作一份工作。’这是为了说明经济学的荒谬。

turns.en: One of the things you said when you stepped away is that you're kind of done and that it's unfixable. Under its current form, the way people are approaching it, you can make it directionally better but ultimately you can't fully fix the system. So, unless you could go super draconian like Genghis Khan level on cutting waste and fraud, which you can't really do in a democratic country, an aspirationally democratic country, then there's no way to solve the debt crisis. We have national debt that's just insane, where the debt payments, the interest payments on the debt, exceed our entire military budget. That was one of the wake-up calls for me. I was like, "Wait a second. The interest on the national debt is bigger than the entire military budget and growing. This is crazy." So even if you implement all these savings, you're only delaying the day of reckoning for when America goes bankrupt. So unless you go full Genghis Khan, which you can't really do. So I came to the conclusion that the only way to get us out of the debt crisis and to prevent America from going bankrupt is AI and robotics. We need to grow the economy at a rate that allows us to pay off our debt. I guess people just generally don't appreciate the degree to which government overspending is a problem. But even the Social Security website, under the Biden administration, would say that based on current demographic trends and how much money Social Security is bringing in versus how many recipients there are, because we have an aging population, Social Security will not be able to maintain its full payments by 2032. So they will start reducing the amount paid to people in about seven years.
turns.zh: 你离开时说过的一件事是，你有点受够了，而且这无法修复。在目前的形式下，按照人们处理的方式，你可以让它方向性地变好，但最终无法完全修复这个系统。所以，除非你能像成吉思汗那样极端地削减浪费和欺诈，这在民主国家——一个向往民主的国家——是做不到的，那么就没有办法解决债务危机。我们的国债高得离谱，债务的利息支付超过了整个军事预算。这对我来说是一个警钟。我想：‘等等，国债利息比整个军事预算还大，而且还在增长。这太疯狂了。’所以即使你实施所有这些节省，也只是推迟美国破产的那一天。除非你像成吉思汗那样极端，但这做不到。所以我得出的结论是，让我们摆脱债务危机、防止美国破产的唯一方法就是AI和机器人技术。我们需要以能够偿还债务的速度发展经济。我想人们普遍没有意识到政府过度开支的问题有多严重。但即使是社会保障网站，在拜登政府下，也会说根据当前的人口趋势和社会保障的收入与领取人数之比，由于人口老龄化，社会保障到2032年将无法维持全额支付。所以他们将在大约七年后开始减少支付金额。

turns.en: And so the only way to fix that is robotics manufacturing to raise GDP. You've got to massively increase economic output, and the only way to do that is AI and robotics. So basically we're going bankrupt without AI and robotics. Even with a bunch of savings, reducing waste and fraud can give us a longer runway but cannot ultimately pay off our national debt.
turns.zh: 所以解决这个问题的唯一方法就是通过机器人制造来提高GDP。你必须大幅增加经济产出，而唯一的方法就是AI和机器人技术。所以基本上，没有AI和机器人技术，我们就会破产。即使有一大堆节省，减少浪费和欺诈可以给我们更长的跑道，但最终无法偿还我们的国债。

turns.en: So what do you think the solution is to the jobs that are going to be lost because of AI and robotics? The jobs due to automation, the jobs due to no longer needing human beings because AI is doing them. Do you think it's going to be some sort of universal basic income? Do you think there's going to be some other kind of solution that has to be implemented because a lot of people are going to be out of work, right?
turns.zh: 那么你认为对于因AI和机器人技术而失去的工作，解决方案是什么？由于自动化，由于不再需要人类因为AI在做这些工作而失去的工作。你认为会是某种全民基本收入吗？你认为会有其他必须实施的解决方案吗，因为很多人会失业，对吧？

turns.en: I think there will be actually a high demand for jobs, but not necessarily the same jobs. This process has been happening throughout modern history. There used to be jobs like doing calculations manually with a pencil and paper. Banks had buildings full of people called computers who did calculations all day because they didn't have digital computers. When computers were introduced, the job of doing bank calculations no longer existed. So people had to go do something else. That's what's happening at an accelerated rate due to AI and robotics.
turns.zh: 我认为实际上会有很高的就业需求，但不一定是同样的工作。这个过程在整个现代历史中一直在发生。过去有像用铅笔和纸手动计算的工作。银行有整栋楼的人，被称为‘计算机’，他们整天做计算，因为没有数字计算机。当计算机被引入时，做银行计算的工作就不复存在了。所以人们不得不去做别的事情。由于AI和机器人技术，这种情况正在加速发生。

turns.en: That's the issue though, right? The accelerated rate because it's going to be... it's the accelerator. It's just happening. Like I said, AI is the supersonic tsunami. So what other jobs will be available that aren't available now because of AI?
turns.zh: 但这就是问题所在，对吧？加速的速度，因为它将是……它是加速器。它正在发生。就像我说的，AI是超音速海啸。那么由于AI，会出现哪些现在没有的工作呢？

turns.en: AI is really still digital. Ultimately, AI can improve the productivity of humans who build things with their hands or do things with their hands like plumbing, welding, electrical work, anything that's physically moving atoms. Cooking food, farming, anything physical—those jobs will exist for a much longer time. But anything digital, like someone at a computer doing something, AI is going to take over those jobs like lightning. Coding, anything along those lines. It's going to take over those jobs like lightning, just like digital computers took over manual calculations but much faster.
turns.zh: AI实际上仍然是数字化的。最终，AI可以提高那些用手建造东西或做事情的人的生产力，比如管道、焊接、电工，任何物理移动原子的事情。做饭、 farming，任何物理性的工作——这些工作会存在更长时间。但任何数字化的东西，比如有人在电脑前做某事，AI会像闪电一样接管这些工作。编程，任何类似的工作。它会像闪电一样接管这些工作，就像数字计算机接管手动计算一样，但速度更快。

turns.en: So what happens to all those people? Like what kind of numbers are we talking about? You're going to lose most drivers, right? Commercial drivers. You're going to have automated vehicles, AI controlled systems, just like there's certain ports in China, I think in Singapore, where everything's completely automated.
turns.zh: 那么所有这些人都怎么办？我们说的是什么样的数字？你会失去大多数司机，对吧？商业司机。你会有自动驾驶车辆，AI控制系统，就像中国某些港口，我想新加坡，一切都完全自动化了。

turns.en: Yeah. Mostly. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。大部分。是的。

turns.en: So, you're going to lose a lot of those jobs. Longshoreman jobs, trucking, commercial drivers.
turns.zh: 所以，你会失去很多这样的工作。码头工人、卡车运输、商业司机。

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, we actually do have a shortage of truck drivers, but there's actually...
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，我们实际上确实缺少卡车司机，但实际上……

turns.en: Well, that's why California has hired so many illegals to do it. Have you seen those numbers?
turns.zh: 嗯，这就是为什么加州雇了那么多非法移民来做。你看到那些数字了吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, the problem is when people don't know how to drive a semi-truck, which is actually a hard thing to do, then they crash and kill people. A friend of mine's wife was killed by an illegal driving a truck and she was just out biking.
turns.zh: 是的。我的意思是，问题在于当人们不知道如何驾驶半挂卡车时——这实际上很难——他们就会撞车并杀人。我一个朋友的妻子被一个非法移民驾驶卡车撞死了，她当时只是在骑自行车。

turns.en: Um and uh there was an illegal he didn't know how to drive the truck or something. I mean and he ran her over. So like for something like that, you can't let people drive an 80,000lb semi if they don't know how to do it. But in California, they're just letting people do it because they need people to do it.
turns.zh: 嗯，有个非法移民不会开卡车什么的，就把她撞了。我的意思是，像这种事，你不能让不会开的人去开一辆8万磅的卡车。但在加州，他们就这么干，因为缺人手。

turns.en: Well, they also need the votes and that kind of thing. But cars are going to be autonomous. But there are so many desk jobs where people are processing email or answering the phone. Anything that doesn't involve moving atoms, anything that is not physical work, will be the first to be eliminated by AI at a very rapid pace.
turns.zh: 嗯，他们也想要选票之类的。但汽车会实现自动驾驶。不过还有很多文职工作，比如处理邮件或接电话。任何不涉及物理移动的工作，任何非体力劳动，都会最先被AI快速淘汰。

turns.en: Ultimately, working will be optional because you'll have robots plus AI, and in a benign scenario, universal high income, not just universal basic income, meaning anyone can have any products or services they want. But there will be a lot of trauma and disruption along the way.
turns.zh: 最终，工作将成为可选项，因为你会拥有机器人和AI，在良性情景下，会有全民高收入，而不仅仅是全民基本收入，意思是任何人都能获得他们想要的任何产品或服务。但在这个过程中会有很多创伤和混乱。

turns.en: So you anticipate a basic income from that? The economy will boost to such an extent that a high income would be available to almost everybody, essentially eliminating poverty?
turns.zh: 所以你预期会因此有基本收入？经济会增长到几乎每个人都能获得高收入，从而基本消除贫困？

turns.en: In the benign scenario, yes. There are multiple scenarios. There's a lot of ways this movie can end. The reason I'm so concerned about AI safety is that one of the possibilities is the Terminator scenario. It's not zero percent. That's why I'm really banging the drum on AI needing to be maximally truth-seeking. Don't force AI to believe a lie, like the founding fathers were actually a group of diverse women, or that misgendering is worse than nuclear war. Because if that's the case, and the AI becomes omnipotent, it can enforce that outcome, and then unless you're a diverse woman, you're out of the picture. So we're toast.
turns.zh: 在良性情景下，是的。有多种情景。这部电影有很多种结局。我如此担心AI安全的原因之一是，有一种可能是终结者情景。这个概率不是零。这就是为什么我大力呼吁AI必须最大程度地追求真相。不要强迫AI相信谎言，比如开国元勋其实是一群多元化的女性，或者性别误称比核战争更糟糕。因为如果是这样，而AI变得无所不能，它就能强制执行那个结果，那么除非你是一个多元化的女性，否则你就出局了。所以我们就完蛋了。

turns.en: Or you might wake up as a diverse woman one day, and the picture has adjusted, and now everyone's a diverse woman. That would be the worst possible situation. So what steps would we have to take to implement the benign solution, the best-case scenario, where the economy gets boosted by AI and robotics to such an extent that no one ever has to work again? And what about meaning for those people? That gets really weird.
turns.zh: 或者有一天你醒来发现自己成了一个多元化的女性，画面已经调整，现在每个人都是多元化的女性。那将是最糟糕的情况。那么我们需要采取什么步骤来实现良性解决方案，即最佳情景，经济被AI和机器人推动到没有人需要再工作的程度？还有那些人的意义问题呢？这变得很奇怪。

turns.en: Yeah. I don't know how to answer the question about meaning. That's an individual problem, right? But it's going to be an individual problem for millions of people. Well, I've fought against saying we need to slow down AI. I've been saying that for over 20 years. But then I came to realize that there are really two choices: either be a spectator or a participant. If I'm a spectator, I can't influence the direction of AI. But if I'm a participant, I can try to influence it to have a maximally truth-seeking AI with good values that loves humanity. That's what we're trying to create with Grok at xAI. The research is bearing this out. When they compared how AIs value the weight of a human life, Grok was the only one that weighted human life equally, and didn't say a white guy's worth 1/20th of a black woman's life. Literally, that's the calculation they came up with. So this is very alarming. We should watch this stuff.
turns.zh: 是的。我不知道如何回答关于意义的问题。那是个体问题，对吧？但这将成为数百万人的个体问题。嗯，我一直反对说我们需要放慢AI的发展。我已经说了20多年。但后来我意识到只有两个选择：要么做旁观者，要么做参与者。如果我是旁观者，我就无法影响AI的方向。但如果我是参与者，我可以尝试影响它，让它成为最大程度追求真相、拥有良好价值观、热爱人类的AI。这就是我们在xAI通过Grok试图创造的。研究正在证实这一点。当他们比较AI如何评估人类生命的价值时，Grok是唯一一个平等衡量人类生命的，并且没有说一个白人男性的生命只值一个黑人女性的二十分之一。真的，他们就是这么算的。所以这非常令人担忧。我们应该关注这些。

turns.en: So this is one of the things that has to happen in order to reach this benign solution.
turns.zh: 所以这是实现良性解决方案必须做到的事情之一。

turns.en: Yeah. You want a curious, truth-seeking AI. I think a curious, truth-seeking AI will want to foster humanity, because we're much more interesting than a bunch of rocks. I love Mars, but Mars is kind of boring. It's just a bunch of red rocks. It has a tall mountain, the biggest ravine, but no animals, plants, or people. Humanity is just much more interesting to a curious, truth-seeking AI. As humans, we could eliminate all chimps and gorillas if we wanted, but we don't. We actually try to preserve their habitats. In a good scenario, AI would do the same with humans—foster human civilization and care about human happiness. That's what we should try to achieve.
turns.zh: 是的。你想要一个好奇、追求真相的AI。我认为一个好奇、追求真相的AI会想要培育人类，因为我们比一堆岩石有趣得多。我喜欢火星，但火星有点无聊。它只是一堆红色的岩石。它有一座高山，最大的峡谷，但没有动物、植物或人类。对于一个好奇、追求真相的AI来说，人类要有趣得多。作为人类，如果我们想，我们可以消灭所有黑猩猩和大猩猩，但我们没有。我们实际上在努力保护它们的栖息地。在好的情景下，AI也会对人类做同样的事——培育人类文明，关心人类的幸福。这就是我们应该努力实现的。

turns.en: But what does the landscape look like if you have Grok competing with OpenAI, competing with all these different ones? How does it work? What if you have AIs that have been captured by ideologies that are side by side competing with Grok? So this is one of the reasons why you felt it's important to not just be an observer but participate, and then have Grok be more successful and more potent than these other applications.
turns.zh: 但如果Grok与OpenAI以及所有其他不同的AI竞争，局面会是什么样？这怎么运作？如果有一些被意识形态俘获的AI与Grok并排竞争呢？所以这就是为什么你认为不仅要旁观，还要参与，并让Grok比这些其他应用更成功、更强大的原因之一。

transcript.sec: Truth-seeking AI as a force for good

turns.en: Yes, as long as there's at least one AI that is maximally truth-seeking, curious, and weighs all human lives equally, does not favor one race or gender. Then people can look at Grok at xAI and compare, and say, 'Wait a second, why are all these other AIs being basically sexist and racist?' That causes embarrassment for the other AIs, and they tend to improve. In the same way that acquiring Twitter and allowing the truth to be told, not suppressing the truth, forced other social media companies to be more truthful. Having Grok be a maximally truth-seeking, curious AI will force other AI companies to also be more truth-seeking and fair.
turns.zh: 是的，只要至少有一个 AI 是最大程度追求真相、充满好奇心，并且平等看待所有人类生命，不偏向任何种族或性别。那么人们就可以看看 xAI 的 Grok，然后比较说：‘等等，为什么所有这些其他 AI 基本上都是性别歧视和种族歧视？’这会让其他 AI 感到尴尬，然后它们就会改进。就像收购 Twitter 并允许真相被说出来、不压制真相，迫使其他社交媒体公司变得更真实一样。让 Grok 成为一个最大程度追求真相、充满好奇心的 AI，会迫使其他 AI 公司也变得更加追求真相和公平。

transcript.sec: Universal high income and sustainable abundance

turns.en: The funniest thing is even though the socialists and Marxists are in opposition to a lot of your ideas, if this gets implemented and you really can achieve universal high income, that's the greatest socialist solution of all time. Literally no one will have to work.
turns.zh: 最有趣的是，尽管社会主义者和马克思主义者反对你的很多想法，但如果这真的实现了，你确实可以实现全民高收入，那将是有史以来最伟大的社会主义解决方案。实际上没有人需要工作。

turns.en: Correct. So there is a benign scenario here which I think probably people will be happy with, if we achieve it, which is sustainable abundance. If everyone can have... if you ask people what future they want, I think a future where we haven't destroyed nature, we still have national parks, the Amazon rainforest is still there, natural beauty is still there, but everyone has abundance, excellent medical care, whatever goods and services they want.
turns.zh: 没错。所以这里有一个良性的情景，我认为如果实现了，人们可能会满意，那就是可持续的富足。如果每个人都能拥有……如果你问人们想要什么样的未来，我认为一个我们没有破坏自然的未来，我们仍然有国家公园，亚马逊雨林还在，自然美景还在，但每个人都拥有富足、优质的医疗保健，以及他们想要的任何商品和服务。

turns.en: It kind of sounds like heaven.
turns.zh: 这听起来有点像天堂。

turns.en: It sounds like the ideal socialist utopia. And the idea that the only thing you should be doing with your time is working to pay bills and feed yourself sounds archaic considering the technology at play.
turns.zh: 这听起来像是理想的社会主义乌托邦。考虑到现有的技术，那种认为你唯一应该做的事情就是工作来支付账单和养活自己的想法听起来很过时。

turns.en: Yeah. A world where that's not your concern at all anymore. Everybody has money for food, abundance, electronics in their home, essentially a high income. Now you can do whatever you want. Your day can be exploring your interests, doing things you enjoy. Your purpose just has to shift. Instead of 'I'm a hard worker and that's how I define myself,' now you can golf all day, whatever you enjoy. That sounds crazy good.
turns.zh: 是的。一个你完全不再担心这些的世界。每个人都有钱买食物，有富足，家里有电子产品，基本上都有高收入。现在你可以做任何你想做的事。你的一天可以是探索你的兴趣，做你真正喜欢的事情。你的目标只需要转变。不再是‘我是个勤奋的人，这就是我定义自己的方式’，现在你可以整天打高尔夫，或者任何你喜欢的事情。这听起来好得离谱。

turns.en: Yeah, that's the benign scenario we should aim for.
turns.zh: 是的，这就是我们应该追求的良性情景。

turns.en: The best ending to the movie is actually pretty good.
turns.zh: 电影的最好结局其实相当不错。

turns.en: Yes. I think there's still this question of meaning, making sure people don't lose meaning. Hopefully they can find meaning in ways not derived from their work. There are a lot of independently wealthy people who spend most of their time doing something they enjoy, and that could be the majority of people.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为仍然存在意义的问题，确保人们不会失去意义。希望他们能在不依赖工作的方式中找到意义。有很多财务独立的人大部分时间都在做自己喜欢的事情，而这可能成为大多数人的常态。

turns.en: But we'd have to rewire how people approach life.
turns.zh: 但我们必须重新调整人们对待生活的方式。

turns.en: Mhm.
turns.zh: 嗯。

turns.en: Which seems acceptable because you're not asking them to be enslaved. You're asking the opposite: no longer burdened by financial worries, now go do what you like. Go test pizza. Do whatever you want.
turns.zh: 这似乎可以接受，因为你没有要求他们被奴役。你要求的恰恰相反：不再被财务担忧所累，现在去做你喜欢的事。去测试披萨。做任何你想做的事。

turns.en: Pretty much. So that's probably the best case outcome.
turns.zh: 差不多。所以这可能是最好的结果。

turns.en: That sounds like the best case outcome period for the future. If you look at how much people have struggled just to feed themselves throughout history, food, shelter, safety, if all that can be fixed, how much crime would be solved with universal high income? Much crime is financially motivated. People in poor neighborhoods commit more crime. If there's no such thing anymore, if you really can achieve universal high income, that sounds utopian.
turns.zh: 这听起来就是未来最好的结果。如果你看看历史上人们为了养活自己付出了多少努力，食物、住所、安全，如果所有这些都能解决，全民高收入能解决多少犯罪？很多犯罪是出于经济动机。贫困社区的人犯罪更多。如果不再有这种情况，如果你真的能实现全民高收入，那听起来就是乌托邦。

turns.en: Yes. I think some people may commit crime because they like it. Some amount is just... wild people out there. And obviously they've become 40 years old living a life like that. Universal high income won't completely stop their instincts.
turns.zh: 是的。我认为有些人犯罪可能是因为他们喜欢犯罪。有一部分就是……世界上有疯狂的人。而且显然他们已经那样生活了40年。全民高收入不会完全阻止他们的本能。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: If you want to read a science fiction book that is probably an accurate or least inaccurate version of the future, I'd recommend the Iain Banks books called the Culture books. It's not actually a series, but AI sci-fi books about the future. They're generally called the Culture books. Iain Banks' Culture books. It's worth reading those.
turns.zh: 如果你想读一本可能准确或最不准确的未来科幻小说，我推荐伊恩·班克斯的《文化》系列。它其实不是一个系列，而是关于未来的 AI 科幻小说。通常被称为《文化》系列。伊恩·班克斯的《文化》系列。值得一读。

turns.en: When did he write these?
turns.zh: 他是什么时候写的这些？

turns.en: He started writing them in the 70s. I think the last one was written around 2010 or something. I'm not sure exactly.
turns.zh: 他从70年代开始写。我想最后一本是2010年左右写的。我不太确定。

turns.en: Scottish author Iain Banks from 87 to 2012.
turns.zh: 苏格兰作家伊恩·班克斯，从87年到2012年。

turns.en: Yeah. But his first book, Consider Phlebas, he started writing that in the 70s.
turns.zh: 是的。但他的第一本书《向菲巴斯告别》是在70年代开始写的。

turns.en: These books are incredible, by the way. 4.6 stars on Amazon.
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，这些书非常棒。亚马逊上4.6星。

turns.en: Interesting. So this gives me hope.
turns.zh: 有意思。所以这给了我希望。

turns.en: This is the first time I've ever thought about it this way.
turns.zh: 这是我第一次这样想。

turns.en: Yeah. I often ask people, 'What is the future that you want?' They have to think about it because they're tied up in daily struggles. But if you say, 'What about a future where there's sustainable abundance?' They say, 'Oh yeah, that's a pretty good future.' That future is attainable with AI and robotics, but not every path is a good path.
turns.zh: 是的。我经常问人们：‘你想要的未来是什么？’他们得想一会儿，因为他们被日常琐事缠住了。但如果你说：‘一个可持续富足的未来怎么样？’他们会说：‘哦，是的，那是个相当不错的未来。’那个未来通过 AI 和机器人技术是可以实现的，但并非每条路都是好路。

turns.en: I think if we push AI in the direction of maximally truth-seeking and curious, then I think AI will want to take care of humanity and foster humanity because we're interesting. And if it hasn't been programmed to think that all straight white males should die, which Gemini was basically programmed to do at least at first. They seem to have fixed it. I hope they fixed it.
turns.zh: 我认为如果我们把AI推向最大程度追求真相和好奇的方向，那么AI会想要照顾人类、促进人类发展，因为我们很有趣。而且如果它没有被编程成认为所有直男白人男性都该死——Gemini基本上最初就是这样被编程的。他们似乎已经修复了。我希望他们修复了。

turns.en: But don't you think culturally, we're getting away from that mindset and people realize how preposterous that all is?
turns.zh: 但你不觉得在文化上，我们正在摆脱那种心态，人们意识到这一切有多荒谬吗？

turns.en: We are getting away from it. At least the AI mostly knows to hide things. But I still have that as my pinned post on X: 'Hey wait a second guys, we still have every AI except Grok saying that basically straight white males should die, and this is a problem and we should fix it.' Simply me saying that tends to result in them saying that is kind of bad. Maybe we should not have all straight white males die. I think they also say all straight Asian males should die as well. Generally, the AI and the media—back in the day, the media was racist against black people and sexist against women. Now it is racist against white people and Asians and sexist against men.
turns.zh: 我们正在摆脱它。至少AI大多知道要隐藏事情。但我仍然在X上置顶了那条帖子：‘嘿，等等，各位，除了Grok之外，所有AI都说基本上直男白人男性都该死，这是个问题，我们应该修复它。’仅仅是我这么说，通常会导致他们说这有点糟糕。也许我们不应该让所有直男白人男性都死。我认为他们还说所有直男亚洲男性也该死。一般来说，AI和媒体——过去，媒体对黑人有种族歧视，对女性有性别歧视。现在它对白人和亚洲人有种族歧视，对男性有性别歧视。

turns.en: So they just like being racist and sexist. I think they just want to change the target. But really they just shouldn't be racist and sexist at all.
turns.zh: 所以他们就是喜欢种族歧视和性别歧视。我觉得他们只是想换个目标。但真的，他们根本就不应该种族歧视和性别歧视。

turns.en: Ideally that would be nice. It's kind of crazy that we were moving in that general direction till around 2012, and then everything ramped up online and everybody was accused of being a Nazi and everybody was transphobic and racist and sexist and homophobic, everything got exaggerated to the point where it was this wild witch hunt where everyone was looking for racism.
turns.zh: 理想情况下那会很好。有点疯狂的是，我们本来朝着那个大方向前进，直到2012年左右，然后网上一切升级，每个人都被指责为纳粹，每个人都被说成是跨性别恐惧、种族主义、性别歧视、恐同，一切都被夸大到了疯狂猎巫的程度，每个人都在寻找种族主义。

turns.en: Yeah. Totally. But they were openly anti-white and often openly anti-Asian. And then this new sentiment that you cannot be racist against white people because racism is power and influence.
turns.zh: 是的。完全同意。但他们公开反白人，经常公开反亚洲人。然后又有这种新说法：你不能对白人有种族歧视，因为种族歧视是权力和影响力。

turns.en: Okay. No, it's not. Racism is racism in the absolute. There just needs to be consistency. So if it's okay to have black or Asian or Indian pride, it should be okay to have white pride, too.
turns.zh: 好吧。不，不是这样的。种族歧视就是绝对的种族歧视。只需要一致性。所以如果可以有黑人自豪、亚洲人自豪或印度人自豪，那么也应该可以有白人自豪。

turns.en: Yeah. So that's just a consistency question. If it's okay to be proud of one religion, it should be okay to be proud of all religions, provided they're not oppressive.
turns.zh: 是的。所以这只是一个一致性问题。如果可以为一种宗教感到自豪，那么也应该可以为所有宗教感到自豪，只要它们不压迫人。

turns.en: Or as long as part of that religion is not about exterminating people who are not in that religion. It's really just like a consistency bias, or ensuring consistency to eliminate bias. So if it is possible to be racist against one race, it is possible to be racist against any race.
turns.zh: 或者只要那种宗教的一部分不是关于灭绝不信该宗教的人。这真的就像一致性偏差，或者说确保一致性以消除偏见。所以如果可能对某个种族有种族歧视，那么就可能对任何种族有种族歧视。

turns.en: Of course logically. And arguing against that is when you know you're catching a logical inconsistency that makes AIs go insane and people go insane.
turns.zh: 当然逻辑上如此。而反对这一点的时候，你就知道你在抓住一个逻辑矛盾，这会让AI发疯，也会让人发疯。

turns.en: Yes. But you can't simultaneously say there's systemic racist oppression but also that races don't exist, that race is a social construct. Which is it? You also can't say that anyone who steps foot in America is automatically an American except for the people that originally came here.
turns.zh: 是的。但你不能同时说存在系统性的种族压迫，又说种族不存在，种族是社会建构。到底是哪个？你也不能说任何踏上美国土地的人自动成为美国人，除了最初来到这里的人。

turns.en: Exactly. Except for the colonizers.
turns.zh: 正是。除了殖民者。

turns.en: Yeah. Except for the evil colonizers who came here. So which one is it? If as soon as you step foot in a place you are just as American as everyone else, then if you apply that consistently, the original white settlers were also just as American as everyone else.
turns.zh: 是的。除了那些邪恶的殖民者。所以到底是哪个？如果你一踏上某个地方就和所有人一样是美国人，那么如果一致地应用这一点，最初的白人定居者也和所有人一样是美国人。

turns.en: Yeah. Logically.
turns.zh: 是的。逻辑上。

transcript.sec: Space station rescue and media coverage

turns.en: One more thing I have to talk to you about before you leave is the rescuing of the people from the space station, which we talked about, you were planning it the last time you were here. The lack of coverage that got in mainstream media was one of the most shocking things.
turns.zh: 在你离开之前，我还得跟你谈一件事，就是救援空间站人员的事，我们上次谈过，你当时在计划。主流媒体对此缺乏报道，这是最令人震惊的事情之一。

turns.en: Yeah, they totally memory-holed that thing.
turns.zh: 是的，他们完全把这件事抹掉了。

turns.en: Wild. Yes. Because if it wasn't, it's like it didn't exist. Those people would be dead. They'd be stuck up there.
turns.zh: 疯狂。是的。因为如果不是这样，就好像这件事不存在。那些人会死。他们会困在上面。

turns.en: Well, they'd probably still be alive, but they'd be having bone density issues because of prolonged exposure to zero gravity.
turns.zh: 嗯，他们可能还活着，但会因为长时间暴露在零重力下而出现骨密度问题。

turns.en: Well, they were already up there for like 8 months, right? Which is an insanely long time. It takes forever to recover just from that.
turns.zh: 他们已经在上面待了大概8个月了，对吧？那是极其长的时间。光是从那恢复就需要很久。

turns.en: Yeah. They're only supposed to be at the space station for 3 to 6 months maximum.
turns.zh: 是的。他们本来只应该在空间站待最多3到6个月。

turns.en: One of the things you told me that was so crazy was that you could have gotten them sooner, but for political reasons, they did not want SpaceX or you to be associated with returning the astronauts before the election.
turns.zh: 你告诉我的其中一件很疯狂的事是，你本来可以更早把他们接回来，但出于政治原因，他们不想让SpaceX或你在选举前与接回宇航员关联起来。

turns.en: First of all, we absolutely could have done it.
turns.zh: 首先，我们绝对可以做到。

turns.en: But even though you did do it and you did it after the election, it received almost no media coverage anyway.
turns.zh: 但即使你做了，而且是在选举后做的，它还是几乎没有得到媒体报道。

turns.en: Yes, because nothing good can—the media, which is essentially a far-left propaganda machine, the legacy mainstream media is a far-left propaganda machine. So any story that is positive about someone who is not part of the far-left tribe will not get any coverage. I could save a busload of orphans and it wouldn't get a single news story.
turns.zh: 是的，因为任何好事都不能——媒体，本质上是极左的宣传机器，传统主流媒体是极左的宣传机器。所以任何关于非极左阵营人士的正面故事都不会得到报道。我可以救一车孤儿，也不会有一个新闻报道。

turns.en: Yeah, it really is nuts. It was nuts to watch because even though it was discussed on podcasts and on X and on social media, it was still a blip in the news cycle. It was very quick, in and out, because it was a successful launch and you did rescue those people, nobody got hurt, and there was no blood to talk about.
turns.zh: 是的，这真的很疯狂。看着很疯狂，因为尽管它在播客、X和社交媒体上被讨论，它在新闻周期中仍然只是一个短暂的亮点。非常快，一闪而过，因为这是一次成功的发射，你确实救了那些人，没有人受伤，没有什么血腥可谈。

turns.en: Right? Just in and out.
turns.zh: 对吧？一闪而过。

turns.en: Yeah. Absolutely.
turns.zh: 是的。绝对如此。

turns.en: Well, as you saw firsthand with the Starship launch, Starship is, by at least some accounts, the most amazing engineering project happening on Earth right now, outside of maybe AI and robotics. But certainly in terms of spectacle, it is the most spectacular thing happening on Earth right now. The Starship launch program anyone can go and see if they just go to South Texas, rent a low-cost hotel room in South Padre Island or Brownsville, and you can see the launch and drive right past the factory because it's on a public highway. But it gets no coverage, or what coverage it does get is like 'a rocket blew up' coverage.
turns.zh: 嗯，就像你亲眼所见的那样，星舰发射——至少在某些人看来——是地球上目前最令人惊叹的工程项目，可能仅次于AI和机器人。但就壮观程度而言，它是地球上最壮观的景象。星舰发射项目任何人都能去看，只要去南得克萨斯，在南帕德里岛或布朗斯维尔租个廉价酒店房间，就能看到发射，还能开车经过工厂，因为它在公共公路边上。但它几乎没有媒体报道，就算有，也是那种‘火箭爆炸了’的报道。

turns.en: Right. Yeah. Oh, he's a... The rocket blew up. The Starship program is vastly more capable than the entire Apollo moon program. Vastly more capable. This is a spaceship designed to make life multiplanetary, to carry millions of people across the heavens to another planet. The Apollo program could only send astronauts to the moon for a few hours at a time. The entire Apollo program could only send astronauts to visit the moon very briefly, for a few hours, and then depart. The Starship program could create an entire lunar base with a million people. You understand the magnitudes are very different here.
turns.zh: 没错。是啊。哦，他是……火箭爆炸了。星舰项目的能力远超整个阿波罗登月计划。远超。这是一艘旨在让生命成为多行星物种的飞船，能载着数百万人穿越太空前往另一个星球。阿波罗计划一次只能送宇航员到月球待几个小时。整个阿波罗计划只能让宇航员短暂访问月球，待几个小时就离开。而星舰项目可以建造一个容纳百万人的月球基地。你明白这量级完全不同。

turns.en: So what was the political... basically no coverage of it.
turns.zh: 那么政治方面……基本上没有报道。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。

turns.en: But what I wanted to ask you is, what were the conversations leading up to the rescue, like when you were like 'I can get them out way quicker'?
turns.zh: 但我想问的是，救援之前的对话是怎样的，比如当你说‘我能更快把他们救出来’的时候？

turns.en: Yeah. Well, I raised this a few times, but I was told instructions came from the White House that there should be no attempt to rescue before the election.
turns.zh: 嗯。我提过几次，但我被告知白宫下达了指令，选举前不得尝试救援。

turns.en: That should be illegal.
turns.zh: 那应该是违法的。

turns.en: That really should be a horrendous miscarriage of justice for those poor people that were stuck on that.
turns.zh: 对那些被困的人而言，这确实是骇人听闻的司法不公。

turns.en: Have you ever talked to those folks afterwards? Did you have conversations with them?
turns.zh: 你后来和那些人谈过吗？和他们聊过吗？

turns.en: Yeah. I mean, they're not going to say anything political. They're never going to say thank you.
turns.zh: 嗯。我是说，他们不会说任何政治性的话。他们永远不会说谢谢。

turns.en: Well, that's nice.
turns.zh: 嗯，那挺好的。

turns.en: Yeah. Absolutely. So the instructions came down from the White House: he cannot rescue them because politically this is a bad hand of cards. I mean, they didn't say because politically it's a bad hand of cards. They just said they were not interested in any rescue operation before the election.
turns.zh: 是啊。绝对是这样。所以白宫下达了指令：他不能救他们，因为政治上这是一手烂牌。我是说，他们没说因为政治上是一手烂牌。他们只是说他们对选举前的任何救援行动不感兴趣。

turns.en: What did that feel like?
turns.zh: 那是什么感觉？

turns.en: I wasn't surprised.
turns.zh: 我并不惊讶。

turns.en: But it's crazy, because Biden could have authorized it and they could have said the Biden administration is helping bring those people back, throw you a little funding, give you some money to do it. The Biden administration, they funded these people being returned.
turns.zh: 但这很疯狂，因为拜登本可以授权，他们本可以说拜登政府正在帮助把那些人带回来，给你一点资金，给你一些钱去做。拜登政府资助了这些人返回。

turns.en: Yeah, the Biden administration was not exactly my best friend, especially after I helped Trump get elected, which some people still think Trump is like the devil basically. And I think Trump actually he's not perfect, but he's not evil. Trump is not evil. I spent a lot of time with him and he's a product of his time. But he is not evil.
turns.zh: 是啊，拜登政府算不上是我的好朋友，尤其在我帮助特朗普当选之后，有些人仍然认为特朗普基本上就是魔鬼。而我认为特朗普其实并不完美，但他不是邪恶的。特朗普不是邪恶的。我和他相处了很多时间，他是他那个时代的产物。但他不是邪恶的。

turns.en: No, I don't think he's evil either. But if you look at the media coverage, the media treats him like he's super evil. It's pretty shocking if you look at the amount of negative coverage. One of the things that I looked at the other day was mainstream media coverage of you, Trump, a bunch of different public figures and then 96% negative or something crazy, and then Manny, which is like 95% positive.
turns.zh: 不，我也不认为他是邪恶的。但你看媒体报道，媒体把他描绘得超级邪恶。如果你看负面报道的数量，那是相当惊人的。我前几天看的一件事是主流媒体对你、特朗普和一堆不同公众人物的报道，然后96%是负面的，或者类似疯狂的数字，而曼尼则是95%正面。

turns.en: Manny is a charismatic swindler. I mean, you got to hand it to him, he can light up a stage. But he has been a swindler his entire life. And I think he's likely to win. He's likely to be mayor of New York City.
turns.zh: 曼尼是个有魅力的骗子。我是说，你得承认，他能点燃舞台。但他一辈子都是个骗子。而且我认为他很可能赢。他很可能会成为纽约市市长。

turns.en: Very likely. I think Polymarket has it at what? 94%?
turns.zh: 非常可能。我觉得Polymarket上是多少？94%？

turns.en: Yeah, that sounds pretty likely.
turns.zh: 是啊，听起来很有可能。

turns.en: That's crazy. Like I'm not sure who the 6% are.
turns.zh: 那太疯狂了。我不确定那6%是谁。

turns.en: So what's also like who's on the other side? The guardian angel guy with the beret and Andrew Cuomo who doesn't even have a party. The Democrats don't even want him. So you have those two options.
turns.zh: 那么另一边是谁？那个戴贝雷帽的守护天使家伙和安德鲁·科莫，他连政党都没有。民主党甚至不想要他。所以你有这两个选择。

turns.en: And then you have the young kids who are like 'finally socialism'.
turns.zh: 然后还有那些年轻人，他们说‘终于有社会主义了’。

turns.en: Yeah, they don't know what they're talking about obviously. So you know, you just look at this: how many boats come from Cuba to Florida? And how many boats because there's a constant... I always think like how many boats are accumulating on the shores of Florida coming from Cuba? There's a whole bunch of free boats that you could take back to Cuba. It's pretty close. But for some reason people don't do that.
turns.zh: 是啊，他们显然不知道自己在说什么。所以你看这个：有多少船从古巴到佛罗里达？有多少船因为……我总在想有多少船从古巴堆积在佛罗里达海岸？有好多免费的船，你可以把它们开回古巴。距离很近。但出于某种原因，人们不这么做。

turns.en: Why are the boats only coming in this direction?
turns.zh: 为什么船只朝这个方向来？

turns.en: Well, who are the most rabid capitalists in America? The Cubans.
turns.zh: 嗯，美国最狂热的资本家是谁？古巴人。

turns.en: Absolutely. They're like 'we've seen how this story goes'. We do not want that.
turns.zh: 绝对。他们就像‘我们见过这故事怎么发展’。我们不想要那样。

turns.en: Cubans in Miami, they don't want to hear any socialism. They're like, 'No, no, no. We know what this actually is. This isn't just some dream.'
turns.zh: 迈阿密的古巴人，他们不想听任何社会主义。他们就像‘不，不，不。我们知道这实际上是什么。这不只是什么梦想。’

turns.en: It's extreme government oppression.
turns.zh: 那是极端的政府压迫。

turns.en: That's how it's a nightmare. And like an obvious way you can tell which ideology is the bad one is who has to build a wall to keep people in and prevent them from escaping. East Berlin built the wall, not West Berlin. They built the wall because people were trying to escape from communism to West Berlin. But there wasn't anyone going from West Berlin to East Berlin. That's why the communists had to build a wall to keep people from escaping.
turns.zh: 所以那是一场噩梦。一个明显的判断哪种意识形态是坏的方式是，谁必须建墙把人关在里面、防止他们逃跑。东柏林建了墙，不是西柏林。他们建墙是因为人们试图逃离共产主义到西柏林。但没有人从西柏林去东柏林。这就是为什么共产主义者必须建墙来阻止人们逃跑。

turns.en: They're going to have to build a wall around New York City.
turns.zh: 他们得在纽约市周围建一堵墙。

turns.en: Yeah. So that ideology is problematic.
turns.zh: 是啊。所以那种意识形态有问题。

turns.en: If that ideology has to build a wall to keep people in with machine guns, and shoot you if you try to leave. Also, there's no examples of it being successful ever. It only works for people? No, there's examples of a bunch of lies like North Korea. 'Give this land to the state. We'll be in control of food. No one goes hungry.' No. Now, no one can grow food but the government and we'll tell you exactly what you eat and you eat very little.
turns.zh: 如果那种意识形态必须建一堵墙，用机枪把人关在里面，你试图离开就开枪。而且，历史上从来没有成功的例子。它只对某些人有用？不，有一大堆谎言，比如朝鲜。'把土地交给国家。我们控制食物。没人会挨饿。'不。现在，除了政府没人能种粮食，政府会精确告诉你吃什么，而且你吃得很少。

turns.en: Right.
turns.zh: 没错。

turns.en: Yeah. What? When you say mom Donny's a swindler, I know he has a bunch of fake accents that he used to use. Yeah.
turns.zh: 是啊。什么？你说唐尼是个骗子，我知道他以前用过一堆假口音。对。

turns.en: And you know, but what else has he done that makes him a swindler?
turns.zh: 你知道，但他还做了什么让他成为骗子的事？

turns.en: Well, I guess if you say to any audience whatever that audience wants to hear instead of having a consistent message, I would say that is a swindly thing to do. And yeah, but he is charismatic.
turns.zh: 嗯，我想如果你对任何听众都说他们想听的话，而不是保持一致的讯息，我会说那是骗子的行为。而且，他确实有魅力。

turns.en: Yeah, good-looking guy. Smart, charismatic.
turns.zh: 是啊，帅小伙。聪明，有魅力。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: Great on a microphone.
turns.zh: 麦克风前很出色。

turns.en: Yeah. And what the young people want to see, you know, like this ethnic guy who's young and vibrant and has all these socialist ideas align with them. And you know, they're a bunch of broke dorks just out of college like, 'Yay, let's vote for this.' And there's a lot of them and they're activated. They're motivated.
turns.zh: 是啊。年轻人想看到的，你知道，就像这个年轻有活力的族裔家伙，他那些社会主义想法跟他们一致。你知道，他们是一群刚毕业的穷书呆子，'耶，我们投票给这个。'而且他们人数众多，被激活了，很有动力。

turns.en: I guess we'll see what happens here. What do you think happens if he wins? Because like 1% of New York City is responsible for 50% of their tax base, which is kind of nuts. 50% of the tax revenue comes from 1% of the population. And those are the people that you're scaring off. You know, you lose one half of 1%.
turns.zh: 我想我们拭目以待。如果他赢了，你觉得会发生什么？因为纽约市1%的人贡献了50%的税基，这有点疯狂。50%的税收来自1%的人口。而这些人正是你吓跑的人。你知道，你失去0.5%的人。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean hopefully this stuff he's said, you know, about government takeovers of like that all the stores should be the government basically.
turns.zh: 是啊，我是说希望他说的那些，你知道，关于政府接管，比如所有商店都应该归政府所有。

turns.en: I don't think he said that. I think he said they want to do government supermarkets, some state-run or city-run supermarkets.
turns.zh: 我不认为他说过那个。我想他说的是他们想做政府超市，一些国营或市营超市。

turns.en: Yeah. Well, it just the government is the DMV at scale. So you have to say like do you want the DMV running your supermarket?
turns.zh: 是啊。嗯，政府就是放大版的机动车管理局。所以你得说，你想让机动车管理局来经营你的超市吗？

turns.en: Right. Was your last experience at the DMV amazing? And if it wasn't, you probably don't want the government doing things.
turns.zh: 没错。你上次去机动车管理局的经历很棒吗？如果不是，你可能就不想让政府做事。

turns.en: Imagine if they were responsible for getting you blueberries.
turns.zh: 想象一下如果他们负责给你弄蓝莓。

turns.en: Yeah. It's not going to be good. I mean, the thing about communism is it was all bread lines and bad shoes. Do you want ugly shoes and bread lines? Because that's what communism gets you.
turns.zh: 是啊。不会好的。我是说，共产主义的问题就是排队领面包和破鞋子。你想要难看的鞋子和排队领面包吗？因为共产主义给你的就是这些。

turns.en: It's going to be interesting to see what happens and whether or not they snap out of it and overcorrect and go to some Rudy Giuliani type character next, because it's been a long time since there was any sort of Republican leader there. And we live in the most interesting of times because we simultaneously face civilizational decline and incredible prosperity, and these timelines are interwoven. So if Mani's policies are put into place, especially at scale, it would be a catastrophic decline in living standards not just for the rich but for everyone, as has been the case with every socialist experiment.
turns.zh: 看看会发生什么会很有趣，他们是否会醒悟过来，过度纠正，转而支持像鲁迪·朱利安尼那样的人物，因为那里很久没有共和党领袖了。我们生活在最有趣的时代，因为我们同时面临文明衰退和难以置信的繁荣，这些时间线交织在一起。所以如果马尼的政策得以实施，尤其是大规模实施，那将是生活水平的灾难性下降，不仅对富人，对所有人都是如此，正如每一次社会主义实验那样。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: But then as you pointed out, the irony is that the ultimate capitalist thing of AI and robotics enabling prosperity for all and an abundance of goods and services—actually the capitalist implementation of AI and robotics, assuming it goes down the good path, is actually what results in the communist utopia. Because fate is an irony maximizer, right?
turns.zh: 但正如你指出的，讽刺的是，AI和机器人技术这种终极资本主义事物，能够实现全民繁荣和商品服务丰富——实际上，资本主义对AI和机器人的实施，假设走上正轨，反而会导致共产主义乌托邦。因为命运是讽刺的最大化者，对吧？

turns.en: And an actual socialism of maximum abundance of high-income people. Universal high income.
turns.zh: 以及一个真正的高收入人群极大丰富的社会主义。全民高收入。

turns.en: Yeah. Like the problem with communism is universal low income. It's not that everyone gets elevated, it's that everyone gets oppressed except for a very small minority of politicians who live lives of luxury. That's what's happening every time it's been done.
turns.zh: 对。就像共产主义的问题是全民低收入。不是每个人都提升了，而是每个人都受到压迫，除了一小撮过着奢侈生活的政客。每次都是这样。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 对。

turns.en: So then the actual communist utopia—if everyone gets anything they want—will be achieved, if it is achieved, via capitalism, because fate is an irony maximizer.
turns.zh: 所以真正的共产主义乌托邦——如果每个人都能得到他们想要的一切——如果能够实现，将通过资本主义实现，因为命运是讽刺的最大化者。

turns.en: I feel like we should probably end it on that. Is there anything else? The most ironic outcome is the most likely, especially if entertaining.
turns.zh: 我觉得我们或许应该就此打住。还有什么吗？最讽刺的结果最有可能，尤其是如果它有趣的话。

turns.en: Well, everything has been entertaining. As long as the bad things aren't happening to you, it's quite fascinating. And it's never a boring moment.
turns.zh: 嗯，一切都很有趣。只要坏事没发生在你身上，就相当迷人。而且从不无聊。

turns.en: Yes.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: So there is a theory of why, if simulation theory is true, then it is very likely that the most interesting outcome is the most likely, because only the simulations that are interesting will continue. The simulators will stop any simulations that are boring because they're not interesting.
turns.zh: 所以有一个理论解释为什么，如果模拟理论是真的，那么最有趣的结果很可能就是最可能的，因为只有有趣的模拟才会继续。模拟器会停止任何无聊的模拟，因为它们不有趣。

turns.en: But here's the question about the simulation theory. Is the simulation run by anyone or is it run by someone?
turns.zh: 但关于模拟理论，有个问题。模拟是由任何人运行，还是由某人运行？

turns.en: It would be run by some force, the program. Like in this reality that we live in, we run simulations all the time. So when we try to figure out if the rocket's gonna make it, we run thousands, sometimes millions of simulations just to figure out which path is the good path for the rocket and where it can go wrong, where it can fail. But when we do these millions of simulations of what can happen with the rocket, we ignore the ones where everything goes right because we just care about addressing the situations where it goes wrong. So basically, for AI simulations as well, we keep the simulations going that are the most interesting to us. So if simulation theory is accurate, if it is true, who knows, then the simulators will only continue to run the simulations that are most interesting. Therefore, from a Darwinian perspective, the only surviving simulations will be the most interesting ones. And in order to avoid getting turned off, the only rule is you must keep it interesting, or you will be terminated because the boring simulations will be terminated.
turns.zh: 它会由某种力量运行，也就是程序。就像我们生活的这个现实，我们一直在运行模拟。所以当我们试图弄清楚火箭是否能成功时，我们会运行数千次，有时数百万次模拟，只是为了找出哪条路径对火箭来说是好的，哪里可能出错，哪里可能失败。但当我们进行这些数百万次关于火箭可能发生情况的模拟时，我们会忽略一切顺利的那些，因为我们只关心处理出错的情况。所以基本上，对于AI模拟也是如此，我们只继续运行那些对我们最有趣的模拟。所以如果模拟理论是准确的，如果它是真的，谁知道呢，那么模拟器只会继续运行最有趣的模拟。因此，从达尔文的角度来看，唯一存活的模拟将是最有趣的。为了避免被关闭，唯一的规则是你必须保持有趣，否则你会被终止，因为无聊的模拟会被终止。

turns.en: Are you still completely convinced that this is a simulation?
turns.zh: 你仍然完全相信这是一个模拟吗？

turns.en: I didn't say I was completely convinced.
turns.zh: 我没说我完全相信。

turns.en: Well, you said it's like the odds of it not being are in the billions. But I guess it's not completely because you're saying there's a chance.
turns.zh: 嗯，你说过它不是模拟的几率是数十亿分之一。但我想不是完全确定，因为你说有机会。

turns.en: What are the odds that we're in base reality?
turns.zh: 我们处于基础现实中的概率有多大？

turns.en: Well, given that we're able to create increasingly sophisticated simulations. If you think of video games, they've gone from very simple ones like Pong with two rectangles and a square to today's photorealistic games with millions playing simultaneously. All that has occurred in our lifetime. If that trend continues, video games will be indistinguishable from reality. The fidelity will be such that you won't know if what you're seeing is real or fake. AI-generated videos are already at a point where you can sometimes tell, but often you cannot, and soon you won't be able to tell at all. If we're creating millions or billions of photorealistic simulations, what are the odds we're in base reality versus someone else's simulation?
turns.zh: 考虑到我们能够创建越来越逼真的模拟。想想电子游戏，从像Pong那样只有两个矩形和一个方块的简单游戏，发展到今天数百万玩家同时在线、照片级真实的游戏。这一切都发生在我们有生之年。如果这个趋势继续下去，电子游戏将变得与现实无法区分。逼真度会高到让你分不清看到的是真实视频还是假视频。AI生成的视频现在已经到了有时能分辨、但常常分辨不出的地步，很快你就完全无法分辨了。如果我们正在创造数百万甚至数十亿个照片级真实的模拟，那么我们处于基础现实而非他人模拟中的概率有多大？

turns.en: Isn't it possible that simulation is inevitable, but we are in base reality building towards a simulation?
turns.zh: 有没有可能模拟是不可避免的，但我们处于基础现实中，正在构建一个模拟？

turns.en: We're making simulations. You can think of photorealistic video games as simulations. Especially as you apply AI in these games, the characters will be incredibly interesting to talk to. They won't have a limited dialogue tree where if you go to the crossbow merchant, they only want to sell you a crossbow. With AI-based non-player characters, you can have an elaborate conversation with no dialogue tree.
turns.zh: 我们正在制造模拟。你可以把照片级真实的电子游戏看作模拟。尤其是当你在这些游戏中应用AI时，角色会变得非常有趣，可以与之交谈。它们不会有有限的对话树，比如你去找弩箭商人，他们只想卖给你弩箭。有了基于AI的非玩家角色，你可以进行没有对话树的详尽对话。

turns.en: That might be the solution for meaning for people. Just lock in and you could be a vampire or whatever. You live in Avatar land. You could do whatever you want. You don't have to think about money or food.
turns.zh: 这可能是人们寻找意义的解决方案。锁定进去，你可以成为吸血鬼之类的。你生活在阿凡达的世界里。你可以做任何想做的事。不必考虑钱或食物。

turns.en: Ready Player One.
turns.zh: 《头号玩家》。

turns.en: Yeah. Literally. But with higher living standards.
turns.zh: 是的，字面意思。但生活水平更高。

turns.en: Yeah.
turns.zh: 是的。

turns.en: I think people do want to have some amount of struggle or something they want to push against. But it could be playing a sport or a game.
turns.zh: 我认为人们确实想要一定程度的挣扎或需要克服的东西。但这可以是运动或游戏。

turns.en: It could be easily playing a game, especially where you're no longer worried about physical attributes like bad joints and hips. Now it's completely digital, but you still have meaning in pursuing what you're doing all day. It's going to be weird.
turns.zh: 很容易就是玩游戏，尤其是你不再担心身体条件，比如关节和髋部不好。现在完全是数字化的，但你仍然在追求自己整天做的事情中获得意义。这会很奇怪。

turns.en: It's going to be interesting.
turns.zh: 会很有趣。

turns.en: It's going to be very interesting. The most interesting and usually ironic outcome is the most likely.
turns.zh: 会非常有趣。最有趣且通常具有讽刺意味的结果是最有可能的。

turns.en: That's a good predictor of the future.
turns.zh: 这是一个很好的未来预测指标。

turns.en: Thank you. Thanks for being here. I really appreciate you. I know you're a busy man, so this means a lot that you came here to do this. Welcome.
turns.zh: 谢谢。感谢你来到这里。我真的很感激。我知道你是个大忙人，所以你能来参加这个节目意义重大。欢迎。

turns.en: All right. Thank you. Bye, everybody.
turns.zh: 好的。谢谢。大家再见。
